title: The Laws of Connections
content:
## Overall structure

|Part|Focus|Chapters / “Laws”|Flow of ideas|
|---|---|---|---|
|**I – Building Social Bonds**|Why we underestimate our likeability and how to spark new ties|Laws 1‑7|Explains cognitive biases (e.g., _liking gap_, _novelty penalty_) → offers lab‑tested conversation tools (e.g., _fast‑friends procedure_) → ends with norms for praise and vulnerability.|
|**II – Maintaining & Repairing Bonds**|How to deepen, protect, and rekindle relationships|Laws 8‑13|Moves from emotional attunement (_confelicity_, gratitude) to conflict repair (forgiveness, apologies) and finally to reviving “dormant ties.”|

---

## Part I – _Building Social Connections_

### Law 1 – Be Consistent: avoid the “frenemy effect”

_Inconsistent warmth produces cortisol spikes in recipients and erodes trust._ ([Toby Sinclair](https://www.tobysinclair.com/post/summary-the-laws-of-connection-by-david-robson?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Summary: The Laws of Connection by David Robson"))

### Law 2 – Create Mutual Understanding

_Shared inner experiences trump superficial similarities._ Anchored in the **Shared Reality** framework (Epley & colleagues). ([Toby Sinclair](https://www.tobysinclair.com/post/summary-the-laws-of-connection-by-david-robson?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Summary: The Laws of Connection by David Robson"))

### Law 3 – Trust the _Liking Gap_

People systematically **under‑estimate** how much conversation partners like them; correcting this bias boosts approach behaviour. ([The Wall Street Journal](https://www.wsj.com/arts-culture/books/the-laws-of-connection-review-stronger-together-b54fe560?utm_source=chatgpt.com "'The Laws of Connection' Review: Stronger Together"))

### Law 4 – Swap “Perspective‑Taking” for “Perspective‑Getting”

Instead of guessing others’ thoughts, **ask follow‑up questions** and paraphrase (_active attention_). Backed by experiments in _Psychological Science_ showing doubled accuracy in mind‑reading scores. ([WIRED](https://www.wired.com/story/the-science-of-having-a-great-conversation-research-social-connection?utm_source=chatgpt.com "The Science of Having a Great Conversation"))

### Law 5 – Design Better Conversations

Combines three lab tools:

- **[[Fast‑Friends Procedure]]** (36 escalating questions; Aron 1997)
    
- **Follow‑up question ratio** (ideal ≈50 %)
    
- **Novelty penalty**—listeners prefer the familiar, so blend old & new topics. ([WIRED](https://www.wired.com/story/the-science-of-having-a-great-conversation-research-social-connection?utm_source=chatgpt.com "The Science of Having a Great Conversation"))
    

### Law 6 – Praise Specifically, Not Vaguely

Specific compliments trigger larger striatal reward responses in fMRI than generic praise; Robson cites studies at Columbia Business School. ([The Wall Street Journal](https://www.wsj.com/arts-culture/books/the-laws-of-connection-review-stronger-together-b54fe560?utm_source=chatgpt.com "'The Laws of Connection' Review: Stronger Together"))

### Law 7 – Show Your “Beautiful Mess”

Revealing vulnerability raises likeability more than we predict (_Beautiful Mess Effect_). “People warm to your cracks, not your polish.” ([British Psychological Society](https://www.bps.org.uk/psychologist/beautiful-mess-effect?utm_source=chatgpt.com "The beautiful mess effect | BPS - British Psychological Society"))

---

## Part II – _Maintaining & Repairing Connections_

### Law 8 – Practice **Con­felicity**

Celebrate friends’ successes out loud; social psychologists find it strengthens perceived closeness more than sympathy in bad times. ([The Times](https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/laws-of-connection-david-robson-review-bhwv7n85b?utm_source=chatgpt.com "The Laws of Connection by David Robson review - the secret to a long life"))

### Law 9 – Use the _Find‑Remind‑Bind_ Cycle of Gratitude

Expressing thanks **finds** new allies, **reminds** both parties of value, and **binds** future cooperation. Backed by Algoe (2012) longitudinal data. ([The Times](https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/laws-of-connection-david-robson-review-bhwv7n85b?utm_source=chatgpt.com "The Laws of Connection by David Robson review - the secret to a long life"))

### Law 10 – Apologise & Forgive Promptly

Delayed apologies lose ~50 % of their restorative power in meta‑analysis of 54 studies; the book offers a four‑step template (acknowledge, express regret, offer amends, commit change). ([Toby Sinclair](https://www.tobysinclair.com/post/summary-the-laws-of-connection-by-david-robson?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Summary: The Laws of Connection by David Robson"))

### Law 11 – Ask for Help

People _overestimate_ the burden of help requests; compliance rates in experiments often exceed 50 %. Robson argues the act affirms social worth for both parties. ([The Times](https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/laws-of-connection-david-robson-review-bhwv7n85b?utm_source=chatgpt.com "The Laws of Connection by David Robson review - the secret to a long life"))

### Law 12 – Maintain “Social Homeostasis”

Humans self‑regulate contact like sleep or hunger. Simple check‑ins (texts, voice notes) prevent isolation spirals—especially important post‑pandemic. ([Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Laws-Connection-Scientific-Secrets-Building/dp/1639366482 "The Laws of Connection: The Scientific Secrets of Building a Strong Social Network: Robson, David: 9781639366484: Amazon.com: Books"))

### Law 13 – Revive Dormant Ties

Reaching out to lapsed contacts yields **stronger informational value** than talking to current close friends (Levin & Cross study, 2004). Robson supplies email scripts and timing tips. ([Toby Sinclair](https://www.tobysinclair.com/post/summary-the-laws-of-connection-by-david-robson?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Summary: The Laws of Connection by David Robson"))

---

## Key frameworks, models & methodologies

|Concept|Essence|Where used|
|---|---|---|
|**Liking Gap**|We think others like us ~30 % less than they do|Laws 3, 5|
|**Fast‑Friends Procedure**|36 questions → intimacy in <45 min|Law 5|
|**Beautiful Mess Effect**|Admitted flaws ↑ likability|Law 7|
|**Find‑Remind‑Bind**|Gratitude’s three social functions|Law 9|
|**Social Homeostasis**|Optimal “connection set‑point”|Law 12|
|**Amae** (Japanese)|Comfortable dependence in close ties|Threaded through Part II|

([davidrobson.me](https://davidrobson.me/books/the-laws-of-connection/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "The Laws of Connection - David Robson"))

---

## Evidence base & data highlights

- **Mortality‑risk meta‑analysis:** strong social ties cut all‑cause mortality by **50 %** (Holt‑Lunstad 2010). ([PMC](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC2910600/?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Social Relationships and Mortality Risk: A Meta-analytic Review - PMC"))
    
- **Conversation studies:** follow‑up questions double perceived empathy. ([WIRED](https://www.wired.com/story/the-science-of-having-a-great-conversation-research-social-connection?utm_source=chatgpt.com "The Science of Having a Great Conversation"))
    
- **Gratitude emails:** senders ** underestimate** recipient happiness by ~40 % (Algoe). ([The Times](https://www.thetimes.co.uk/article/laws-of-connection-david-robson-review-bhwv7n85b?utm_source=chatgpt.com "The Laws of Connection by David Robson review - the secret to a long life"))
    
- **Dormant ties revival:** 67 % of managers gained new solutions from old contacts vs 43 % from current network (Levin & Cross). ([Toby Sinclair](https://www.tobysinclair.com/post/summary-the-laws-of-connection-by-david-robson?utm_source=chatgpt.com "Summary: The Laws of Connection by David Robson"))
    

---

## Notable quotations (≤ 90 chars each)

1. “Connection is a biological necessity, not a luxury.” ([Amazon](https://www.amazon.com/Laws-Connection-Scientific-Secrets-Building/dp/1639366482 "The Laws of Connection: The Scientific Secrets of Building a Strong Social Network: Robson, David: 9781639366484: Amazon.com: Books"))
    
2. “People like you more than you think—act on that assumption.” ([The Wall Street Journal](https://www.wsj.com/arts-culture/books/the-laws-of-connection-review-stronger-together-b54fe560?utm_source=chatgpt.com "'The Laws of Connection' Review: Stronger Together"))
    
3. “Your rough edges are invitations, not warnings.” ([British Psychological Society](https://www.bps.org.uk/psychologist/beautiful-mess-effect?utm_source=chatgpt.com "The beautiful mess effect | BPS - British Psychological Society"))
    

---

## Synthesis

Robson’s central contribution is to **translate cutting‑edge social‑psychology and neuroscience into 13 actionable “laws.”** Collectively they dismantle three pervasive myths: that likability is fixed, that conversation is talent not skill, and that old relationships fade for good. By pairing bias‑correcting insights (e.g., _liking gap_) with evidence‑backed techniques (e.g., _fast‑friends_), he shows connection is **malleable and measurable**. In an era of record loneliness, the book reframes friendship as an “everyday health intervention” on par with diet or exercise, offering readers a concrete playbook for cultivating richer, longer‑lasting bonds.

---

[[The 13 Laws]]

title: The 13 Laws
content:
## Part I – Building New Connections

|#|Law|What it Means in Plain English|How to Put It into Practice|
|---|---|---|---|
|**1**|**Be Consistent**|People feel safe when your warmth and tone don’t swing up and down. Mixed signals (friendly one day, distant the next) set off alarm bells.|Make greetings and responses steady. If you’re busy, say so rather than disappearing; predictable honesty beats accidental coldness.|
|**2**|**Create Mutual Understanding**|The fastest route to closeness is sharing what’s happening inside your heads—thoughts, feelings, little observations—not just swapping facts.|Look for chances to say “That’s exactly how I feel” or “Here’s what was going through my mind.” Even tiny overlaps (“I hate when that happens too”) build a shared inner world.|
|**3**|**Trust the Liking Gap**|We almost always underestimate how much others like us after a first chat. Realize that your self‑doubt is skewed.|After meeting someone, assume the interaction went better than you fear. This upbeat belief nudges you to follow up instead of retreating.|
|**4**|**Swap Perspective‑Taking for Perspective‑Getting**|Instead of guessing what someone is thinking, **ask** and then listen. Direct questions beat mind‑reading.|Use phrases like “What’s that like for you?” or “Tell me more.” Follow with a short paraphrase (“So you’re saying…”) to prove you heard them.|
|**5**|**Design Better Conversations**|Good chats have a rhythm: trade questions, gradually deepen topics, avoid monologues.|Try the “36 fast‑friends” questions as a template (start light: “If you could invite anyone to dinner…”, end deep: “What’s your greatest fear?”). Aim for a 50‑50 question‑answer split.|
|**6**|**Praise Specifically, Not Vaguely**|“Great job” feels nice; “The way you structured that slide made the numbers pop” feels amazing. Specifics show you truly noticed.|Point to a concrete detail—an effort, a phrasing, a color choice—when you compliment.|
|**7**|**Show Your Beautiful Mess**|Revealing small imperfections (a worry, a goof‑up) makes you relatable. People connect to authenticity, not polish.|Share a minor struggle or silly mistake the next time you tell a story. It signals trust and invites empathy.|

---

## Part II – Strengthening & Repairing Connections

|#|Law|What it Means in Plain English|How to Put It into Practice|
|---|---|---|---|
|**8**|**Practice Confelicity**(“shared joy”)|Celebrate friends’ wins as if they were your own; positive energy bonds more than sympathy alone.|Send a pumped‑up voice note or meme when a friend lands a new job. Ask details, cheer loudly.|
|**9**|**Follow the Find‑Remind‑Bind Cycle of Gratitude**|A thank‑you does three things: attracts allies (find), refreshes memories of goodwill (remind), and cements future support (bind).|Say thanks promptly, describe exactly what helped you, and share how it made a difference.|
|**10**|**Apologize & Forgive Promptly**|Mistakes are inevitable; speed and sincerity in saying sorry keep cracks from widening.|Use four parts: name the wrong, say you’re sorry, offer to fix it, state how you’ll avoid repeating it. When you’re the hurt party, accept genuine apologies early to prevent bitterness.|
|**11**|**Ask for Help**|Most people _want_ to be helpful and feel good when asked. The cost you imagine is higher than the real cost to them.|Be clear (“Could you review this document by Tuesday?”) and explain why you chose them (“Your eye for detail is unmatched”).|
|**12**|**Maintain Social Homeostasis**|Just as your body signals hunger, it signals connection needs—ignore them and you drift toward loneliness.|Schedule quick check‑ins (a two‑minute text or photo) before silence stretches too long. Small touches keep the relationship “oxygen” flowing.|
|**13**|**Revive Dormant Ties**|Old friends or colleagues often provide fresh insight and goodwill; they’re glad you reached out.|Send a brief “Thought of you when…” message with a genuine update or question. Mention shared history to warm things up, then propose a quick call or coffee.|

---

### Big Takeaway

Together, the laws say **connection is a skill set**, not a mystery. Small, deliberate actions—steady warmth, curious questions, concrete praise, quick apologies, joyful cheerleading—stack up to create strong, resilient relationships that boost happiness, health, and opportunity.

title: Fast‑Friends Procedure
content:
Below are the **36 “Fast‑Friends” questions** exactly as they appear in _The Laws of Connection_ (Robson reproduces the original Aron et al. list).  
The questions are asked in three escalating sets; each set invites a little more self‑disclosure than the one before, so partners move from light small‑talk to deeper personal sharing over roughly 45 minutes. ([ggia.berkeley.edu](https://ggia.berkeley.edu/practice/36_questions_for_increasing_closeness "36 Questions for Increasing Closeness | Practice
| Greater Good in Action"))

---

### **Set I – Easy Warm‑ups (Questions 1‑12)**

1. Given the choice of anyone in the world, whom would you want as a dinner guest?
    
2. Would you like to be famous? In what way?
    
3. Before making a telephone call, do you ever rehearse what you are going to say? Why?
    
4. What would constitute a “perfect” day for you?
    
5. When did you last sing to yourself? To someone else?
    
6. If you were able to live to the age of 90 and retain either the mind or body of a 30‑year‑old for the last 60 years of your life, which would you want?
    
7. Do you have a secret hunch about how you will die?
    
8. Name three things you and your partner appear to have in common.
    
9. For what in your life do you feel most grateful?
    
10. If you could change anything about the way you were raised, what would it be?
    
11. Take four minutes and tell your partner your life story in as much detail as possible.
    
12. If you could wake up tomorrow having gained any one quality or ability, what would it be?
    

---

### **Set II – Getting Personal (Questions 13‑24)**

13. If a crystal ball could tell you the truth about yourself, your life, the future, or anything else, what would you want to know?
    
14. Is there something that you’ve dreamed of doing for a long time? Why haven’t you done it?
    
15. What is the greatest accomplishment of your life?
    
16. What do you value most in a friendship?
    
17. What is your most treasured memory?
    
18. What is your most terrible memory?
    
19. If you knew that in one year you would die suddenly, would you change anything about the way you are now living? Why?
    
20. What does friendship mean to you?
    
21. What roles do love and affection play in your life?
    
22. Alternate sharing something you consider a positive characteristic of your partner. Share a total of five items.
    
23. How close and warm is your family? Do you feel your childhood was happier than most other people’s?
    
24. How do you feel about your relationship with your mother?
    

---

### **Set III – Deep Disclosure (Questions 25‑36)**

25. Make three true “we” statements each. For instance, “We are both in this room feeling …”
    
26. Complete this sentence: “I wish I had someone with whom I could share …”
    
27. If you were going to become a close friend with your partner, please share what would be important for them to know.
    
28. Tell your partner what you like about them; be very honest this time, saying things that you might not say to someone you’ve just met.
    
29. Share with your partner an embarrassing moment in your life.
    
30. When did you last cry in front of another person? By yourself?
    
31. Tell your partner something that you like about them _already_.
    
32. What, if anything, is too serious to be joked about?
    
33. If you were to die this evening with no opportunity to communicate with anyone, what would you most regret not having told someone? Why haven’t you told them yet?
    
34. Your house, containing everything you own, catches fire. After saving your loved ones and pets, you have time to safely make a final dash to save any one item. What would it be? Why?
    
35. Of all the people in your family, whose death would you find most disturbing? Why?
    
36. Share a personal problem and ask your partner’s advice on how they might handle it. Also ask your partner to reflect back how you seem to be feeling about the problem you have chosen.
    

---

**How Robson frames them in the book**:

- **Time‑boxed rhythm** – 15 minutes per set keeps partners moving together through the disclosure levels.
    
- **Turn‑taking** – alternating who answers first models reciprocity and prevents one‑sided oversharing.
    
- **Escalation principle** – each question nudges just beyond the previous comfort zone, which Robson notes triggers the “self‑other overlap” that accelerates closeness.
    

Use them verbatim, stick to the order, and remember that mutual openness—not speed—creates the magic.

title: PO in the same positions as NO or NOT
content:
'Wednesday is PO a holiday'

If you have to explain PO to someone who doesn't know it:
1. Challenge function, look at it another way
2. Provocative function
3. Anti-arrogance function, 'don't be so arrogant, so dogmatic'
4. Overreaction, 'let's cool it. There is no point in getting upset about this'

title: Effect of random stimulation
content:
Any two inputs cannot remain separate no matter how unconnected they are. 

The effect of random inputs on the development of a situation:
![[8F0A4B04-2A3C-439A-9878-A5F19819055F.jpeg]]
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Effect of random stimulation.md

title: Rotation of attention
content:
you can try reversing the situation in order to shift attention. Previous eg: instead to produce 1 winner, how many matches do I need to produce 110 losers?

Another method:
List the different features of the situation and then proceed methodically through this list paying attention to each feature in turn. Do not feel that some features are so trivial that they do not deserve any attention. The difficulty is that in any situation one can pick out as many features as one likes since the features reside not in the situation but in the way it is looked at.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Rotation of attention.md

title: Headlines
content: rewrite headlines from newspaper.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Headlines.md

title: Random word stimulation
content:
There is no correct way of using it. Just get bizarre from the word you obtained. The word is used to get things going - not to prove anything. 

Do not immediately look for another random word from the one you obtained, cuz this establish a search routin in which one goes through word after word until one finds a suitable one. 

Give yourself 3 minutes approx to generate sequence of random words/thoughts from the one you obtained.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Random word stimulation.md

title: Challenging labels
content:
Do not ask for the term to be defined. Question the use of the term as a term, not looking for a justification of the term. 

Read an article and write the cliche words you find. The point is not that labels are right or wrong, but that sometimes it is too convenient to use some label to justify something. (Eg: human rights)
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Challenging labels.md

title: Establish new labels
content:
the purpose is to escape the distorting effects of the old labels.

The polarising effect tends to shift information into established categories. The fewer the categories, the greater the shift and distortion. By creating new labels one accept information with less distortion. 

Eg: LT overal with creative thinking. But the latter is surrounded by a complex of meanings including artistic expression, talent, sensitivity, inspiration, ecc, which makes the need of LT to be separate from creative thinking.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Establish new labels.md

title: Design in problem solving
content:
Design is a special case of problem solving. One wants to bring about a desired state of affairs. Design is more open ended than [[Problem solving]].

Design does not have to be a drawing but for the practice of LT it's much more useful.

Explanatory notes may be added but must be brief.

[[Cliche units]]

title: Polarization
content:
It is easier to establish two completely different patterns than to change an established pattern. 
There is a tendency for the established patterns to 'absorb' similar patterns which are treated as repetitions. 
This result in a distortion of information presented. The pattern that would have established by the information is shifted towards an established pattern. If there are two established patterns then the shift may be towards one or the other. 

The apparent nature of the pattern has been shifted to make it fit with the established pattern. 
![[E550894F-A8EE-4ED4-8DCF-D78F7731C9CE.jpeg]]
Once the item is in the box with the corresponding label of the box, it is impossible to tell apart the objects in the box, even though originally they presented differences. 

In order to find appropriate box for any item that does not fit readily into any available box you can:
- Concentrare on those points which show that it should fit into one box.
- Concentrate on those points which show that it should *not* fit into a particular box (eg: characteristic of the box instead of the item)

If two things are similar one can notice the points of similarity and say 'the two things are the same' or one can notice the points of difference and say 'the two things are different'. 
The two things would be shifted together to be similar or shifted apart to be different.
![[5736DC8C-E505-4B87-A7BA-1A6E4A3337DA.jpeg]]

Similarly when there is an established label a new item is either pushed right under that label or else pushed right out. 

From a practical pov, this polarising system is very effective. One can just establish few major categories and then push everything into one of these. Instead to assess everything in detail and then decide how one is going to react, one merely assess whether it fits into one category or another. 
Once the thing has been pushed into a category then reaction is easy since the categories are established and so is the reaction to them. 

###### *New categories*
When does one decide that the item does not fit into any existing category and creates a new one?
The danger of polarization is that things can be shifted around so much that thee never comes a point when a new category *has to be created*. Nor is there any indication as to how many established categories there should be. 

So the main dangers of polarization:
- Once established, categories become permanent.
- New information is altered so that it fits an established category. Once it has done so, there is no indication that it is any different from anything else under that category.
- At no point it is ever essential to create new categories. One can get by with very few.
- The fewer the categories, the greater the degree of shift.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Polarization.md

title: Provocation
content:
The use of arrangements of information as stimuli. Juxtapositions, introduction of random words, abolition of concept division, use of fantasy and nonsense.

This second use simply indicates that the arrangement of info has no justification expcept the possibility that it might set off new lines of thought. *The arrangement is not examined in itself but only in terms of what it sets off*.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Provocation.md

title: Counteracting NO
content:
PO is used to counteract the absolute block caused by NO label.

PO implies 'let us cover up that NO label for the moment and proceed as if it was not there'

Any definite use of the NO label is an invitation to use PO
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Counteracting NO.md

title: Fractionation
content:
If one takes any situation and breaks it down into fractions one can then restructure the situation by putting the fractions together in a new way. 

The aim is restructuring not explanation. 

==True and false divisions==
It's not about the analysis of a situation into its component parts. You do not try to find the true components parts of a situation, you try to create parts. You are not trying to discover the correct way, you look for a provocative arrangement of information. 
Eg apple picking machine, fractions: reaching, finding, picking, transport to ground, undamaged apples. 

==Complete division and overlap==
Since you do not want to provide descriptive analysis it does not matter if the fractions do not cover the whole situation. Does not matter if some fractions overlap. 

==Two unit division==
If you have problems, use this artificial technique. Produce two fractions, then divide those two fractions by 2 again, and so on. 

==Practice== 
Use a design project or any problem and start fractioning.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Fractionation.md

content:
We need some tool to apply lateral thinking, just as NO is a tool for applying logical thinking.

title: Challengin cliché patterns
content:
any patten that is at all useful is a cliché. The more useful it is the more a cliché it tends to become. And the more of a cliché it is the more useful it may become.

PO can be used to challenge any cliché. 
PO not only challenges the way concepts are arranged into patterns but the very concepts themselves. 
'PO freedom' challenges the very concept of freedom not the value or purpose of freedom.
'PO punishment' challenges the ver concept of punishement, not the circumstances under which it is used or the purpose for which it is used.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Challengin cliché patterns.md

title: Attention area
content:
Eg: tennis tournament. Single knockout. 111 participants. What's the minimum number of matches to be arranged?

Instead of starting with the shape of each round (so working with powers of 2), shift the attention area to the losers. If it is single knockout, there must be 110 losers. Losers can lose only 1 match at most --> 110 games at minimum.

Very often in a situation it's not just a matter of the order in which parts are attended to but the choice of parts that are going to be attended to at all. 

To restructure the situation one may need no more than a slight shift in attention. On the other hand if there is no shift in attention it may be very difficult to look at the situation in a different way.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Attention area.md

title: The second function of PO --> challenging old arrangements of information
content:
PO allows one to free from te fixity of established ideas, labels, division, categories, and classification.

Uses of PO for these purposes:
- To challenge the arrogance of established pattens
- To question the validity of established patterns
- To disrupt established patterns and liberate information that can come together to give new patterns
- To rescue e information trapped by the pigeonholes of labels and classifications
- To encourage the search for alternative arrangements of the information

[[Never a judgement]]

[[The response to PO]]

[[Challengin cliché patterns]]

[[Focusing]]

[[Alternatives]]

[[Anti-arrogance]]

[[Counteracting NO]]

[[Anti-division]]

[[Diversion]]

[[PO and overreaction]]
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/The second function of PO --> challenging old arrangements of information.md

title: Myths
content:
Myths are pattern which first arise in the mind. Once these patterns have formed, something may be found in the environment which justifies them or else they dictate the way the environment is looked at. 

Once you have names, you can produce other names. By adding 'un' you can get the opposite. Then look around to see the fit of the word. 
Thing --> label --> opposite --> new thing

Or you can also combine two words to obtain one new. The process is as follows:
![[750FC3CF-0018-4314-9EC2-048529C95C47.jpeg]]

These new units are created on the word level rather than derived from the environment. Yet these myths words are treated in exactly the same way as ordinary words which do refer to actual things in the environment. 

Instead of the myth word following something in the environment, the myth word comes first and produces something in the environment (by dictating the way one looks at something).
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Myths.md

title: Summary - The first function of PO
content:
PO allows one to say anything one like.

With PO one looks forward instead of backwards.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Summary - The first function of PO.md

title: Protection and rescue
content: Holding off judgement. Temporarily reversing judgement. Removal of the NO label.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Protection and rescue.md

title: Blocked by openness
content:
3 ways in which thinking can be blocked.
![[FBC17B3F-4DA3-472D-B20D-5DEE99C6E460.jpeg]]

1. One is blocked by a gap. Cannot proceed further cuz the road runs out. One needs to find more read or to construct a bridge across the river. This is equivalent to having to look around for more information or having to generate some by experiment
2. One is blocked by there being something in the way. Here there is a definite obstacle which bars progress. In order to go on one has to find a way of removing the obstacle or getting round it. Once this has been done progress is easy because the road is there. One can concentrate one's problem solving efforts on overcoming the block.
3. One is blocked because there is nothing in the way. The road is smooth and clear and so one goes shooting past the important side turning unaware that it is even there. 

The third type is what is called *blocked by openness*.

Even when the patterns are adequate this cannot exclude there being very much more effective patterns. 

We have developed methods for dealing with things that are wrong but no methods for dealing with things that are right. 

Cliche patterns strung together constitute the trunk of a straight branching system. As one proceeds smoothly along them one is not even aware that ther are possible side turnings. So if you come to a dead end, you do not know where to go. 

In real life one is always elaborating patterns as if they could only be standard cliche patterns. 

LT is an attempt to find alternative pathways, an attempt to put things together in a new way, no matter how adequate the old way appears to be.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Blocked by openness.md

title: Similarity of PO to other words
content:
PO allows information to be used in ways which are totally unreasonable. With words like hypothesis, possible, suppose and poetry the information is used for its own sake even if the use is tentative.
With PO the info is not used for its own sake but for its effect.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Similarity of PO to other words.md

title: Juxtaposition
content:
To hold two unrelated things together in order to allow them or their asssociations to interact. No connection or relationship at all is implied between the two things. 

Without the PO device one would not easily be able to put things together in this way without finding, suggesting, or forcing some reasons.

Eg: computer PO omelette
Ideas: cooking by computer or by some pre-set automatic device. Central store of recipes and one would use a telephone to dial in your ingredients and requirements in order to be given a matched recipe.
...
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Juxtaposition.md

title: Holding function
content:
PO is used to delay the judgment. 'Let's not analyse whether this is right or wrong - let us see what idea it will lead to'

PO ~ let's hold off evaluation for the moment
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Holding function.md

title: Suspended judgement
content:
In LT one is not so concerned with the nature of an arrangement of information but with where it can lead one. So instead of judging each arrangement and allowing only those that are valid one suspends judgement until later on. It is not a matter of doing without judgement but of deferring it until later. 

The suspension of judgement can:
- make an idea survive longer and make it breed further ideas.
- Make other people offer ideas which their own judgment would have rejected. Such ideas may be extremely useful.
- Make other's ideas be accepted for their stimulating effect instead of being rejected. 
- Make ideas, which are judged to be wrong within the current frame of reference, survive long enough to show that the frame of reference needs altering. 

==Practice==
- do not rush to judge or evaluate ideas. One does not regard judgment as most important thing. One prefers exploration.
- Some ideas are obviously wrong. Shifts attention from why it is wrong to how it can be useful.
- Even if one knows an idea must be eventually thrown out, one delays the moment in order to extract as much usefulness from the idea as possible.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Suspended judgement.md

title: Summary
content:
analogies offere a conveniente method for getting going when one is trying to find new ways of looking at a situation instead of just waiting for inspiration. 

One doesn not start moving only when one can see where is going. One moves for the sake of moving and then sees what happens. 

Analogies have a definite life of their own.

Main usefulness of analogies is as vehicles for functions, processes, and relationships, which can then be transferred to the problem under consideration to help restructure it.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Summary.md

title: Cliche units
content:
cliche units are the standard ways of doing something that are borrowed from another setting. 
Purpose of this exercise is to point out these standard ways of doing things and to show how they may not be the best way. 
In pointing em out one does not have to judge them. Just go further.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Cliche units.md

title: Introduction of a random work
content: Introduce a random unconnected word into a discussion in order to stimulate new ideas.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Introduction of a random work.md

title: PO and overreaction
content:
general function of PO as a laxative to relax the rigidity of a particular way of looking at things. A rigid way can lead to emotional overreaction. In such cases PO acts as a smile or laugh to release tension that accompanies a rigid pov. 

PO acts to lessen the fierce necessity of a particular pov.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/PO and overreaction.md

title: The generation of alternatives
content:
Anti arrogance, relaxation, reexamination, rethinking, restructuring.

PO used to pint out that a particular way of looking at a situation is only one view among many. PO is send to point out that a particular pov appears to be held with an unjustified arrogance. 

1st level: to suggest there may be other ways of looking at situa.
2nd level: to invite restructuring of the situa.

In this context, when putting PO at beginning of a sentence the meaning can become: 'explain what you mean by ...'
However, the function of PO is to ask for a restructuring, to ask for alternative ways of putting things.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/The generation of alternatives.md

title: Alternatives
content:
One goes on generating alternatives up to the point of absurdity - and beyond. 

'It's spring and the bird is on the wing'
'No. The wing is on the bird'
'PO, the bird and the wing both happen to be going along in the same direction'

It must be clear that PO alternatives must not be better arrangements than previous ones, they just need to be different.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Alternatives.md

title: Description - problem solving - design
content:
Established patterns can do three things:
1. Create problems which do not really exists. Such problems are only created by particular divisions, polarizations, conceptualizations.
2. They can act as traits or prisons which prevent a more useful arrangement of information.
3. They can block by adequacy.

There are 3 practical situations which encourage the use of lateral thinking.
- [[Description]]
- [[Problem solving]]
- [[Design in problem solving]]
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Description - problem solving - design.md

title: Function
content:
From the very specific go to the very general. The more specific, the more things you prevent thinking about. 

Pay attention to generating alternative functions and not just ways of carrying out a particular specific and mind closing function. 

Abstraction of a function makes you generate more ideas in the design process.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Function.md

title: Grammatical use of PO
content:
PO protects an arrangement of information from judgement and to indicate that it is being used provocatively.
PO used to challenge a particular arrangement of information such as an idea, a concept or a way of putting things. 

1. [[PO as interjection]]
2. [[PO as preface]]
3. [[PO as juxtaposition]]
4. [[PO in the same positions as NO or NOT]]

title: Dropping labels in discussion
content: Discuss a subject. Then re-explain it without using some particular label used previously.

title: Doubt
content:
Whenever you are blocked by the impossibility of proving a certain point, use PO to upen up things again. 
PO does not prove the point or deny it but it allows the point to be used in any way which will enable the discussion to keep going. Then see what happens. 
It may also be the case that one reach a solution thru the doubtful point and so one comes to realise how vital this point is and therefore increases the effort to prove it.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Doubt.md

title: The use of PO
content:
Uses of PO divide into:
1. [[The generation of alternatives]]
2. [[Provocation]]
3. [[Protection and rescue]]

title: Random stimulation
content:
techniques so far have tried to work from within the idea. Random simulation generate external stimulation which then acts on the idea from outside.

[[Generating random inputs]]

[[Effect of random stimulation]]

[[Random word stimulation]]

==Practice==
State a problem. Generate a random word (dictionary). Write it down with its meaning. How the word can be related to the problem? 

Give different ppl different words to elaborate on. 

Select random word and assign different problems to different ppl

Select random object and try to relate the problem to it. Do not be constrained by the function of the random object.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Random stimulation.md

title: Rephrasing
content:
Practice dropping labels with single sentences. 

Eg of sentences to rewrite:
Children should be as *tidy* as possible in their homework.
Everyone has the right to *equal opportunity* in education.
In a democracy government is by the *will* of the people.

When rephrasing, accept synonyms but stimulate the rephrasing which implies looking at the situation differently.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Rephrasing.md

title: Entry point
content:
divide triangle in 3 parts and rearrange to obtain square or rectangle. It can be quite difficult since the shape of the triangle is not given. It is much easier to start with a square and divide it into 3 parts which can be put together to form a triangle.
![[C86D677E-80D6-4A24-AC8C-86199CD7D040.jpeg]]

The entry point can also be the end. Not the solution end but also a wrong end. Working backwards is effective way.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Entry point.md

title: Labels and discussion
content: set a debate and observe the use of such labels in too convenient a fashion. Become aware of the easy use of these labels.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Labels and discussion.md

title: PO as juxtaposition
content:
When two words are going to be juxtaposed for no reason at all, PO indicates this relationship between them.

PO also involved in the introduction of a random word.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/PO as juxtaposition.md

title: Never a judgement
content:
PO implies 'that arrangement may be the best one - but let us now look around for other ways'

With PO you can bring forward an idea without proving it to be more efficient or suitable, just bring it forward for the sake of it, for the sake of seeing where it'll lead you.

With PO the attention is shifted from 'why' (vertical) to 'where to' (lateral)
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Never a judgement.md

title: Challenge the labels
content:
Why am I using this label? What does it really mean? Is it essential? Is it just a convenient cliche? 

This is challenging directly the use of a label, a word, a name. You don't have to disagree with its use or have a better alternative. Just don't accept the cliche label without challenging it. 

It's not a matter of seeking justification for the label so one can continue using it. 
One continues to challenge the label all the time, even when using it.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Challenge the labels.md

title: Description
content:
Description is a way of making visible the way one understands something - the way one explains that thing to oneself. By having to describe something one has to commit oneself temporarily to a particular pov. This means that one has to generate a definite pov instead of being satisfied with a vague awareness.

The idea of this exercise is to train ppl to realise that there is more than one way of looking at a situation and to be able to generate alternative ways for themselves. For this reason the emphasis is not on the accuracy of the description but on the *difference* between descriptions and on the use of novel methods of description.

The raw material to be described may be picture, photographs, simple geometric outline shapes, written material, story, article. 

The task here is not to generate ideas which have something to do with the material but to just describe that material.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Description.md

title: Problem solving
content:
A problem is the difference between what one has and what one wants.

If description is a matter of looking back to see what one has then problem solving is a matter of looking forward to see what one can get. 

What one wants to bring about may take a variety of forms: 
1. To resolve some difficulty
2. To bring about something new
3. To do away with something unsatisfactory

Problems may be open ended or closed ended. Open ended problems are impossible to try out completely (traffic congestion). They have to be judged not on the base of the results. 
With closed problems there is a definite answer. The solution either works or it does not. 
It is better to find a variety of solutions than to only find the best one. 

There is no harm in reinventing things that have already been invented.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Problem solving.md

title: Focusing
content:
Since the cliche may refer to a particular concept or a phrase or to the whole idea it is helpful if one is specific about what is being challenged by PO.

Eg: 'it is the function of education to train the mind and to pass on to it the knowledge of ages'
One may say ' PO train the mind', 'PO the knowledge', or even 'PO train'

By doing so PO act as a focusing device to direct attention to some concepts that is always taken for granted because there are other concepts which seem more open to reexamination.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Focusing.md

title: Brainstorming
content:
Formal setting for the use of lateral thinking. Take a holiday from vertical thinking and start brainstorming. 

(Having note taker may be good thing)

Main features of a brainstorming sessions are: 
- [[Cross simulation]]
- [[Suspended judgement in brainstorming]]
- [[The formality of the setting]]

... (Check book for further detail)
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Brainstorming.md

title: The first function of PO --> creating new arrangements of information
content:
Experience arranges things in patterns. 
Pattern can be derived from the environment or can be derive from the memory surface of mind.
PO create arrangements of information that do not arise from either of these 2 sources. 
PO used to generate connections that have nothing to do with experience. 

If different arrangements of information could be brought about and held for a short while the the information might snap together to form a new pattern that was either consistent with the background pattern or capable of altering it. 
Purpose of PO is either to bring about arrangements that would otherwise not occur or to protect from dismissal arrangements that would otherwise be dismissed as impossible. 
![[E288C523-201C-4406-B260-C226843564F7.jpeg]]

PO is never a judgment. It allows one to hold an arrangement for a little longer without having to affirm or deny it. 
PO delays judgement. 

With LT an arrangement of info may be wrong in itself but can lead to a perfectly valid new idea. 

If the idea is pursued far Anoush it may be found to make sense. If one holds onto the idea then freshly arrived information can interact with the idea to give a valid idea. 
If the idea is held long enough, then the context into which it did not fit may itself be changed. 
![[8C082E62-3DA0-4DF6-B3F8-A4B960F3B880.jpeg]]

The function of PO is to bring about a provocative arrangement of information without saying anything at all about it. 
The arrangement itself is not important but what happens next is. The purpose of the arrangement is to lead forward to new ideas. 

Practice occasions that are convenient to use PO:
- [[Juxtaposition]]
- [[Introduction of a random work]]
- [[Disconnected jumps]]
- [[Doubt]]
- [[Being wrong]]
- [[Holding function]]
- [[Construction]]

[[Summary - The first function of PO]]
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/The first function of PO --> creating new arrangements of information.md
links: Summary - The first function of PO

title: Generating random inputs
content:
two main ways of bringing about random stimulation:
- [[Exposure]]
- [[Formal generation]]
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Generating random inputs.md

title: PO as preface
content: Used before a sentence or a phrase or a word that is meant to qualify.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/PO as preface.md

title: General function of PO
content:
PO draws attention to the possibility of cliches and rigid ways of looking at things. 

PO indicates that the arrangement of information being made makes sense from a lateral thinking pov even if it does not make sense otherwise. 

PO allows one to make arrangements of information for which there is no justification. 

LT is an holiday from the usual conventions of logic, not an attack upon them. 

As a device PO actually enhances the effectiveness of vertical thinking by keeping it intact. This PO does by providing in a means to bypass vertical thinking in order to introduce a generative factor. Once a new pattern has emerged, it can be developed with the full rigour of vertical thinking and judged.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/General function of PO.md

title: Anti-division
content:
PO also challenges the division which divides something into two separate concepts.
Both an artificial difference and an artificial sameness can be challenged with PO.

PO frees information of labels and allow them to interact.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Anti-division.md

title: The formality of the setting
content:
The more formal the setting the better. The more formal the setting the more change there is of informality in ideas within it. 

Put other people in the condition to say whatever they want/feel
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/The formality of the setting.md

title: Formal generation
content:
Even if we try to get exposed, naturally our mind will start paying attention to what we regard familiar/useful/related to our problem.

To deliberately generate random inputs:
- Use dictionary to provide a random word
- Formal selection of a book or journal in library
- Use of some routin to select an object from the surroundings.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Formal generation.md

title: Words, names, labels
content:
when unit is obtained by dividing up a total situation or putting together other units, fix that unit by giving a separate name. Name established it as a pattern in its own right instead of just being part of another pattern. 

Having a name gives a unit much greater mobility since it now become more sharply divided off from its neighbours and come to exists on its own. 
Without name, the new unit would dissolve back into its separate parts.

Not required to use names to understand, but it is convenient.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Words, names, labels.md

title: The reversal method
content:
Fractionation has some limitations : artificial, fixed patterns. 
In reversal method one takes things as they are and then turns them round, inside out, upside down, back to front. Then see what happens. Provocative rearrangement of information. 
(People should obey government --> govt should obey ppl)

==Different types of reversal==
There is not standard type for reversal, there can be multiple reversal possible given a situation. There is no preferred arrangement, go for the most provocative. 

==Purpose of reversal procedure==
- Escape from necessity to look at situation in the standard way. Does not matter if new way makes sense or not.
- By disrupting original way, one frees information that can come together in a new way.
- To overcome the terror of being wrong.
- Provocative purpose. Reverse --> new position --> see what happened. 

==Practice==
Get some situations and try to reverse them in as many way as possible. Being ridiculous it totally ok.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/The reversal method.md

PO is never a judgment. It allows one to hold an arrangement for a little longer without having to affirm or deny it.

title: Division
content:
the limited attention span means that one only reacts to a bit of the total environment. 
This process may involve dividing up the environment into a number of attention areas. 
Advantages:
- some part of the environment can be reacted to specifically. 
- New and unfamiliar environments can be dealt with picking out features that are familiar. Eventually the situation is explained in terms of such familiar parts.
- Separate parts can be moved around and combined in different ways to produce effects that are not available in the environment.
- It makes communication possible cuz a situation can be described bit by bit instead of as a whole.

*Reassembly*
Units can also be created by putting together other units to form a new one that is then treated as a complete unit.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Division.md

title: Analogies
content:
analogy technique is concerned with generating some movements. 

In argument by analogy one supposes that because something happen in the same way in the analogy then it must happpen in the same way in the problem situation.
In LT analogies are used to generate ideas. 

[[Choosing an analogy]]

==[[Practice]]==

[[Summary]]
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Analogies.md

title: Choice of entry point and attention area
content:
Attention area = part of a situation or problem that is attended to.
Entry point = part of a problem or situation that is *first* attended to. It's the first area of attention of a problem.

Different entry points usually lead to different train of ideas.

[[Entry point]]

[[Attention area]]

[[Rotation of attention]]

==Practice==
Get some problems and list different entry points.

Give same problem to different ppl and give them different Entry points. 

Show a picture and writes down the information that you can get from the pic.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Choice of entry point and attention area.md

title: The mechanism of PO
content:
The bigger the change from the old pattern the more likely is a new pattern to snap together. 'Reasonable' arrangements of information are too closely similar to the old arrangements to give new patterns. 

In emptying a bucket by a si phone the water must first be sucked upwards in the tube. This is an unnatural direction for water to travel. Once the water has reached a certain position then the siphon from and the water will continue to flow naturally out of the bucket until it is empty.
In the same way an unnatural use of information may be necessary to provoke a rearrangement that is itself perfectly natural.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/The mechanism of PO.md

title: The generation of alternative
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/The generation of alternative.md

title: The response to PO
content:
PO does not attack any idea. Is just a challenge to try and think of other ways. 

Just generate as many different ideas as you can. If they all (ultimately) turn out to be worse than the leading one then you have reinforced the leading idea. 
If it happens to pop up another more effective way of looking at the situation then heel goed !

==Practice==
1. PO is never a judgement. PO is never met with a defence of what has been said. Stress the *joint* search for alternatives, not to disprove the leading idea, but just for the sake of looking at it under a different pov.
2. PO may involve the provocative use of information. This means info can be put together in a fantastic and completely unjustified way which is covered by PO. This use of PO must not meet friction, do no generate friction with the new rearrangement of info. Just see where it leads. The response to PO is neither condemnation nor indifference but active cooperation
3. PO may be used as a protection. Hold off judgement. 
4. PO may be a relaxation. When situation has become tense through thee development of rigid points of vies, PO is suggested as a smile to relax the tension. 

Acknowledge that PO is not directed against anything but simply a suggestion for cooperative attempts to restructure a situation.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/The response to PO.md

title: Anti-arrogance
content:
PO is a reminder that the illusion of certainty may be useful but it cannot be absolute. 

PO challenges dogmatism and absolutism. 
PO challenges the arrogance of any absolute statement or judgement or pov.

PO does not imply that the person using it has doubt on the statement. Let alone justified statements. 

PO implies 'you may be right and your logic may be faultless. However, you are starting from perceptions that are arbitrary and you are using concepts that are arbitrary since both are derived from your own individual experience or the general experience of a particular "culture" '

PO is directed at the arrogance surrounding an idea.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Anti-arrogance.md

title: Practice
content:
1. Demonstration
Take a proble, choose analogy, develop analogy and relate it to the problem all along, 

2. Relating an analogy to the problem
Given the problem, develop the analogy and ask for suggestions as to how any particular development in the analogy could be referred to the given problem.

3. Individual effort
Let each person relate the analogy to the problem. 
Look for richness of development in every detail being developed. 

Problem example: finding the way in fog
Example analogies: 
- shortsighted person finding his way around
- Doing crossword puzzle
- Traveller in strange country trying to find the station

4. Same analogy, different problems
Eg analogy: trying to start a car on a cold winter morning
Eg problems: how to tackle difficult math problem, fishing, rescuing cat from high ledge, run race when not stretched up
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Practice.md

title: Design
content:
Design projects can either ask for improvements on existing things or for the actual invention of something to carry out a task. Easier if design do involve something physical since it is easier to solve. Eg: design of new shoes, new classroom ... (concrete objects)
Or also try organisational design: ways of doing things; eg building a house very quickly. 
Eg: apple picking machine, potato peeling machine, cart to go over rough ground, cup that cannot spill, a machine to dig tunnels, device to help cars to park.
Redesign eg: human body, new milk bottle, chair, school, new type of clothes, a better umbrella.
Organisational eg: arrange checkout counters in a supermarket, organise garbage collection, organise shopping to take less time, put a drain across a busy road. 

Designs are to be visuals. Verbal descriptions can be added to explain some features. 
When commenting on results keep this point in mind:
- resist temptation to judge and say it wouldn't work because ...
- Resist temptation to choose one way of doing things as being better than any other.
- Emphasise variety of different ways of carrying out a particular function. 
- Note the features that have been put there for a functional purpose and the ones put there as ornaments.
- Try and look at function underlying a particular design. Try to separate the intention of the designer from the actual way this was carried out.
- Question certain points - not to destroy them but to find if there was any special reason behind them which may not manifest.
- Note the borrowing of complete designs from what might have been seen on television, cinema ...

The idea with every design, if even completely wrong, must be: ok this won't work but what does it lead us to?

You can questions the assumptions behind the designs, use the why technique which can be applied to any part of the design. Eg: apple picking machine; why do we have to *pick*** the apples off the tree? Why does the arm have to go up and down with every apple it picks?
The purpose of why's is not to justify something but to see what happens when one challenges the uniqueness of a particular way of doing things.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Design.md

title: New labels
content:
Have open discussion on what is improperly classified or left out by existing labels.

Try to give a label to 'modi di dire'
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/New labels.md

title: Suspended judgement in brainstorming
content:
Already discussed here: [[Suspended judgement]]

No idea is too ridicolous to be put forward. No attempt at evaluation of ideas has to be made. 
Interrupt any evaluation of ideas. 

Objective of brainstorming is to produce effective ideas.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Suspended judgement in brainstorming.md

title: Diversion
content:
Sometimes the line of development of an idea is so natural and obvious that one moves quite smoothly along this path before ever wondering whether there might be an alternative. 
To prevent this PO may be used as a temporary blocking device. 'That is the natural path of development but we are going to block that path for the moment in order to make it possible to explore some othe pathways'

PO is an excuse for choosing a line of thought that is not the most obvious or the best.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Diversion.md

title: Lateral thinking
content:
Named unit system is highly effective. 
Disadvantage: labels are fixed. Labels alter incoming information instead of new info altering the labels. 
LT wants to break out of clichè patterns and rigid labels are perfect example of it. 
To escape from these labels you can:
- [[Challenge the labels]]
- [[Try and do without them]]
- [[Establish new labels]]
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Lateral thinking.md

title: Limitations of the naming system
content:
Advantage: its permanence
Disadvantage: its permance 
Names labels words are fixed and permanent. Hence the units taken over by these names have also to be fixed and permanent. Hence the patterns which are arrangements of such units tend also to be fixed and permanent.

Even without a name, a concept would be fixed by repeated use and growing familiarity. Putting label on it accelerate the process. 

Some limitations of named unit process:
1. A division at a point of convenience produces two units which then become established and named. Subsequently it may be more convenient to divide the original situation into three units. The establishment of the new units is very difficult as it means carving bits out of the previous units and putting them back to the old units. 
2. If it becomes convenient to change an assembly of unit so that it includes some new units but excludes some old ones this is very difficult
3. When a unit is separated out and named, it's difficult to realise that it's part of a whole
4. When an assembly of units is given an overall name, it may be difficult to realise that it is made up of parts.
5. When division has been made, it's difficult to bridge across that division. If a process has been cut at some point and what goes before is named 'cause' and what comes after is called 'effect' then it is difficult to bridge across the point and call the whole thing 'change'
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Limitations of the naming system.md

title: aLateral thinking - Notes
content:
Lateral thinking involves restructuring, escape and the provocation of new patterns. It is closely related to creativity. But whereas creativity is too often the description of a result, lateral thinking is the description of a process. One can only admire a result but one can learn to use a process. 

In lateral thinking one uses information not for its own sake but for its effect. 

The purpose of thinking is to collect information and to make the best possible us of it. Because of the way the mind works to create fixed concept patterns we cannot make the best use of new information unless we have some means for restructuring the old patterns and bringing them up to date. Vertical thinking is concerned with proving or developing concepts patterns. Lateral thinking is concerned with restructuring such patterns and provoking new ones. 

Even though one had been correct at each stage one would still have to restructure the situation before being able to proceed. 

Disadvantages of information handling system:
1. Patterns tend to become established ever more rigidly since they control attention.
2. It is extremely difficult to change patterns once they have become established.
3. Information that is arranged as part of one pattern cannot easily be used as part of a completely different pattern.
4. There is a tendency towards 'centering' which means that anything which has any resemblance to a standard pattern will be perceived as the standard pattern. 
5. Patterns can be created by divisions which are more or less arbitrary. What is continuous may be divided into distinct units which then grow further apart. Once such units are formed they become self-perpetuating. The division may continue long after it has ceased to be useful or the division may intrude into areas where it has no usefulness. 
6. There is great continuity in the system. A slight divergence at one point can make a huge different later.
7. The sequence of arrival of information plays too important a part in its arrangement. Any arrangement of information is thus unlikely to be the best possible arrangement of the information that is available. 
8. There is a tendency to snap from one pattern to another instead of having a smooth change over. 
9. Even though the choice between two competing patterns may be very fine one of them will be chosen and the other one completely ignored. 
10. There is a marked tendency to polarise. This means moving to either extreme instead of maintaining some balanced point between them.
11. Established patterns get larger and larger. 
12. The mind is a cliché making and cliché using system. 
Purpose of lateral thinking is to overcome these limitations by providing a means for restructuring, for escaping from cliché patterns, for putting information together in new ways to give new ideas.

With vertical thinking one uses the negative in order to block off certain pathways. In lateral thinking there is no negative. There are times when it may be necessary to be wrong in order to be right at the end. 

With vertical thinking one uses information for its own sake in order to move forward to a solution. With lateral thinking one uses information not for its own sake but provocatively in order to bring about repatterning. 

The lateral thinking attitude regards any particular way of looking at things as useful but not unique or absolute. One understand the usefulness of a pattern but instead of regarding it as inevitable one regards it as only one way of putting things together. Lateral thinking is never a judgement. As far as lateral thinking is concerned the only thing that can be wrong with a pattern is the arrogant rigidity with which it is held. 

Information in LT is used not for its own sake but for its effect. One is not interested in the reasons which lead up to and justify the use of a piece of information but in the effects that might follow such a use. 

While acknowledging the usefulness of the patterns created by mind one uses LT to counter arrogance and rigidity. 

The natural inclination is to search for alternatives in order to find the best one. In lateral thinking the purpose of the search is to loosen up rigid patterns and to provoke new patterns. 
Even if the search for alternatives proves to be a waste of time in a particular case it helps develop the habit of looking for alternatives instead of blindly accepting the most obvious approach. 

[[The generation of alternative]]

[[Challenging assumptions]]

[[Suspended judgement]]

links: Choice of entry point and attention area

title: Dominant ideas and crucial factors
content:
one picks out the dominant idea not to be frozen by it but to be able to generate new ideas.
Unless one can pick out the dominant idea one is going to be dominated by it. You need to escape from it. 

Dominant idea resides not in the situation itself but in the way it is looked at. 
The dominant idea may include the whole subject or only one aspect of it. 

==Hierarchy of dominant ideas==
In picking out the DI it is not a matter of having to justify that the idea is the dominant idea which covers the whole situation and which cannot therefore be challenged. It is a matter of picking out an idea which seems (to myself) to dominate the issue. 

==Crucial factor==
DI is the organising theme in a way of looking at a situation. Define it in order to escape from it. 
You must also define the crucial factor. It can immobilise a situation and make it impossible to shift pov. 
Often the CF is an assumption. Once isolated, challenge the necessity for it. 
The challenge of DI and CF are necessary steps to allow use of LT.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Dominant ideas and crucial factors.md

title: Disconnected jumps
content:
In LT one can make disconnected jumps which are not explainable, and then try to fill in the gaps. To indicate that the jump is a lateral disconnected one, preface the comment with PO. 

PO saves one the trouble of having to link the new remark to what has gone before. 
It implies '*Don't look for the reason behind this. Let's just go forward and see what the effect of it is'*
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Disconnected jumps.md

title: NO and PO
content:
Concept of LT is insight restructuring and it's brought about thru rearrangement of information. Rearrangement is the basis of LT and it means escape from rigid patterns established by experience. 
The rearrangement process is incorporated in the concept of the laxative (rearrangement device). It's the means whereby one can escape from established patterns and create new ones. The laxative allows the arrangement of information in new ways from which new patterns can arise. This concept is crystallised in the language tool PO.

PO is to LT what NO is to logical thinking. PO is insight restructuring tool. 

PO is anti-judgement device. NO works within framework of reason. PO works outside that framework. 
PO may be used to produce arrangements which are unreasonable, but they are not really unreasonable because LT functions differently than logical thinking. 

PO provides an escape from the discrete and ordered stability of language which reflects the established patterns of a self-organising memory system. 
PO allows one to:
- Create new patterns (putting information together in new ways and allowing unjustified arrangements of information)
- Challenge old patterns (disrupting them in order to allow the imprisoned information to come together in a new way)
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/NO and PO.md

title: Cross simulation
content:
Fractionation and reversal are methods for getting ideas moving. 
In brainstorming the provocation is supplied also by idea of others. Stimulation may come from the outside. 

Although ideas in a brainstorming session are related to the problem under discussion they can still act as random stimuli for they can be far removed from the idea pattern of the person listening to them.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Cross simulation.md

title: Challenging assumptions
content:
Sometimes a problem cannot be solved by trying different arrangements of the given pieces but only by reexamination of the pieces themselves. 

It is with problem solving that one sets one's own limits within which to explore. If someone else comes along and solves the problem by stepping outside the limits there is an immediate cry of cheating. And yet the limits are usually self imposed. Moreover they are imposed on no stronger grounds than that of convenience. If such boundaries or limits are wrongly set then it may be impossible to solve the problem. 

In challenging assumptions one challenges the necessity of boundaries and limits and one challenges the validity of individual concepts.

Practice
- try to solve some problems/puzzles by breaking the boundaries one think are imposed. Eg: you have to connect all dots with only 4 lines and one assumes that line cannot exceed the grid of dot. Instead you can.
The why technique: start asking why something is the way it is / works the way it works. When answering do not give. Try to avoid feeling that anything is obvious. You need to create discomfort with any explanation. The impression that there is a unique and necessary reason behind everything must be avoided.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Challenging assumptions.md

title: Construction
content: the purpose of changing the problem is not to rephrase it or put it in a better way but to alter it and see what happens next.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Construction.md

title: Try and do without them
content:
We can forget what lies underneath the label. By abolishing them one can rediscover that there is underneath. One may find relevance/irrelevance even if thought the contrary. You may also find the labels should be brought up to date. 

By abolishing labels, one abolishes the cliche convenience of the label. Whenever one comes to a point wen one would normally use the label, one has to find a way of doing without it. This may involve finding another way of looking at things. 
It's not much use substituting some phrase instead of the label but it is still of some use because the phrase can interact with other things in the way a fixed label cannot. 
It is not only in discussing a situation that one tries to do without a particular label, but also in looking at a situation.

If you need to speak or reason without using a label, then one might be able to look at the situation in a different way. One tries to see things as they actually are and not in terms of labels.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Try and do without them.md

title: Dropping labels
content:
How well can you do without a name or label or concept. Drop the label completely and rewrite an article without the use of that label. 

Try to note whether the label has been substituted by another label (cliche phrase) or has caused the thing to be looked at in a different way.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Dropping labels.md

title: PO as interjection
content: Here is used by itself as a reply or interruption, pretty much like NO is used.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/PO as interjection.md

title: Being wrong
content:
In LT you don't mind being wrong on the way to a solution cause it may be necessary to go through a wrong area in order to get to a position from which the correct path is visible. 
PO allows one to move through the wrong area. PO does not make things right, but switches attention from *why something is wrong* to *how it may be useful*

When someone is judging your idea say 'Po [your idea]'

Purpose of PO is to delay judgement, hold the idea in mind for few moments to see what could arise from it instead of dismissing it at once.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Being wrong.md

title: Exposure
content:
1. Accept and welcome random inputs. (Even if irrelevant apparently) Just notice what comes along.
2. Exposure to idea of others. Listen to others, as their thought provide us with random inputs.
3. Exposure to ideas from completely different fields. Talk about the same thing but with someone who tackles it from a radically different field.
4. Physical exposure to random stimulation. Walk around area which contains multitude of different objects and stimulations, even unrelated to the matter you dealing with. 

Main point here is to realise that you are *never looking for anything.*
Do not apply exposure with preset idea in mind of what to expect or what to look for. Just go blank.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Exposure.md

title: Choosing an analogy
content:
analogy doesn't have to fit all along. It can also fit for a moment and then an effort is made to relate it to the problem and from this effort new ways of looking at the problem arise. 

Analogy does not have to be a real life situation . It can be a story provided its development is definite. 

It would be silly to deny the usefulness of something for fear of being harmed by it on some occasions.
file_path: General/Lateral thinking/Choosing an analogy.md
