title: The Debate Between Newton and Leibniz
content:
If we consider all the things of the world at a single time, we see spatial relations among them. The collection of all these spatial relations among the objects of the world at a time is what space is.

The container view of space is fraught with difficulties. Imagine empty space existing and god’s trying to decide where to place the material universe in space. No reason for putting it in one location rather than another. Because every point or region of “space itself” is like every other point or region, there could be no ground for choosing once location for the material world over another. 
Because location in space itself of the material universe cannot have such a sufficient reason, there can be no such thing. 
But the view of space as container and not as mere set of spatial relationships among things, entails the existence of location in space itself. Therefore such a container of space cannot exist.
file_path: Philosophy/Philosophy of Physics/Philosophy of Physics/The Debate Between Newton and Leibniz.md

title: From Space and Time to Spacetime
content:
two events whose interval separation has value zero are such that a light signal in vacuum emitted at one place-time could arrive at the other place-time. 
Here interval is unlike spatial distance in that distinct events can have zero-interval separation. Such events are said to have null or light like separation. 

Events whose interval squared is negative are close enough together in space and far enough apart in time so that signals propagating slower than light can get from one to the other. They are said to have timelike separation. 

Event pairs whose interval squared is positive are too far apart in space and too close together in time for any signal travelling at the speed of light or less to connect them. 
Events which are unconnectable by any causal process are said to have spacelike separation.
file_path: Philosophy/Philosophy of Physics/Philosophy of Physics/From Space and Time to Spacetime.md

title: Intro to Metaphysics
content:
[[Chapter 1 - What is a table?]]

[[Chapter 2 - What is a circle?]]

[[Chapter 3 - Are wholes just sums of parts?]]

[[Chapter 4 - What is a change?]]
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/Notes - Intro to Metaphysics.md
links: Chapter 3 - Are wholes just sums of parts?

title: coming into or going out of existence of something
content:
Change does not occur in the properties of something but to the thing itself. 

Energies not being neither created nor destroyed makes you wonder where it came from in first place. 
In order to say that a change has occurred is a coming into or going out of existence in the relatively weak sense of parts having made a new whole or having being disassembled from a whole. Even tho the parts are still in existence, the whole they were composing is not in existence anymore.

Spatial parts are physical things in space.
What about temporal parts: parts in time? Physical thing existing for some time. Some think they'd be good to explain change. 
Problem is that old Aristotelian theory (things endure through change) different qualities have to be ascribed to one and the same particular. (Qualities that couldn't coexist together in time, tomato being both fully red and fully green, cucumber being 20cm and 30cm)

If you believe in temporal parts, then you can say that the different temporal parts of a thing have these incompatible properties (first temporal part of cucumber being 20cm and second temporal part of cucumber being 30cm)
Analogy with spatial parts: is not weird to think that a part of tomato is green and a part is red. 
Same thing with temporal parts: if there are different parts (spatial or temporal) bearing incompatible properties, we encounter no contradiction. 

Perdurantism
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/coming into or going out of existence of something.md

title: gain or loss of a property
content:
At a point a particular has a property. It loses the property and gain another one. 
--> A change has occurred.
Was it a single swapping of one property for another or two related but distinct events: loss first property and gain of second one?
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/gain or loss of a property.md

title: change within one property
content:
Say a particular has a property and increases or decreases it.

A change occurs and is usually a gradual one (eg: length). 
This change looks like a process. But length is a single property that can come in degrees, magnitudes, or varying quantities. 
There are various determinate lengths that fall under the determinable notion of length. 

So even if a particular can increase or decrease its property, it keeps the same determinable property (of length) but it changes the determinate (lengths), one switching for another.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/change within one property.md

title: Chapter 2 - What is a circle?
content:
There are various individual circles here, appearing at different places and times. There seems to be a something in common to all instances. What is common to them and the other things on the list is what we call circularity. We give this feature a name and treat it as if it is a single kind of entity. The same feature can be found in lots of different places and objects. Some speak of circularity as being a One that runs through Many. In all these many different particulars, the one circularity is to be found.

[[Going round in circles]]

[[Related matters]]

[[Plato's heaven]]

[[It's all just words]]

[[Back down to earth]]
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Chapter 2 - What is a circle?.md

title: Reductionist view
content:
They say: the parts can ultimately explain all the workings of the whole. 

They admit: we do not know all the details yet of how the brain is able to produce consciousness, but have faith that eventually will be able to do so, once discovered all the facts. 

This is philosophical position, not yet proved. 

Facts about the parts determine facts about the wholes.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Reductionist view.md

title: Where's the fun in fundamentalism?
content:
Reductionists think that all ultimately rest on and are explained by physics. 
Or some of the sciences have a degree of independence from each other. 

Take life as example: some try reduce truths of biology to truths of biochemistry and say it's all just about DNA, and DNA is reducible to the truths of chemistry and thus physics. But there are reason why we talk about organisms as wholes rather than sums of parts. 

Natural selection selects fairly high-level properties. An organism as a whole uses its DNA to provide it with what it needs at the level of a whole (long neck of giraffe gives advantage over competitors for food rather than anything related directly to DNA)

So it would not be that microscopic physical facts are determining the macroscopic observable properties of the whole: the other way around! 
--> it's the whole organism that live or die, feed or starve; not their genes or molecules. 

Eyes don't see: we use them to see. Our body parts can be described as living and organic, but they are not organisms, they would not be capable of life if detached from the whole. 

[[Holism]]
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Where's the fun in fundamentalism?.md

title: Bundles of properties
content:
The bundle view is that particulars can be accounted for in terms only of properties.

If a thing were just a collection of properties, it couldn’t survive any change. If one property were lost and another gained, we would have a different collection: for I am assuming that what makes a collection the same thing at different times is that it is composed of the same component things.

Consequently, two collections are different if the things collected within them are different.

How can a particular be just a collection of properties when they change all the time?
we should think of a thing as a series of bundles of properties, united by a degree of continuity.

while the bundles of properties come and go, a particular thing is a succession of such bundles with an appropriate continuity running throughout.

To account for particular objects such as a table, a chair, a dog, and a tree, we had two kinds of ingredients. We had a thing’s properties and its substratum. But with this new bundle theory, it seems that we need only one kind of thing. We just have the properties and, when they come in a bundle or a continuous sequence of such bundles, we say that we thereby have a particular object. So where we previously needed two elements, we now have only one. Another way of looking at this is to say that the notion of substratum has been reduced away entirely in other terms.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Bundles of properties.md

title: The whole truth
content:
I can take a stone off my pile and put it in some other pile. 
But the phone is more of an integrated whole. If I pull the top half, the bottom half moves with it. I could break it apart, but at that point it just become another pile of parts and until then it has a unity that the pile of stones does not have. 

We distinguish between substances (the integrated wholes) and mere aggregates. 

If if replace some stones in the pile, I have a different pile cos we assume that the identity of an aggregate is determined by what the parts are.
If we have different sets of parts, we have different aggregates, even tho some parts may at different times be members of different aggregates. 

If I replace the cover of my phone it does not become a different phone (in sense of numerical identity; see: [[The more things change, the more they stay the same]]). 
Substances can survive a change in their parts that a mere aggregate of parts cannot. 

Can an ability of the whole be explained entirely in terms of the parts? (Especially in case the ability is not shared by the parts)

It seems to me (Francesco) that the way properties of the parts are arranged explains the emergence of new unseen properties of the whole. 

Some qualities are thought of as so special that they emerge at a certain higher level of reality and nothing like them is to be found at the level of the parts.

Is the mental something that is entirely explicable in terms of the physical, with brains in particular the focus of attention? Or is the mental a whole new special kind of quality that emerges only at a certain level of nature?

[[Reductionist view]]

[[Emergentist view]]
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/The whole truth.md
links: The more things change, the more they stay the same

title: Parts all the way down
content:
There is no especially strong evidence for any form of atomism. 

All the complexes must rest on something that is not complex. We have seen that the evidence of observation cannot back this up, because there may be hidden parts that are too small to observe. Unaided reason seems not to dictate the view either. Would it be a contradiction to suppose that the world contained infinite complexity, with ever-smaller parts? There seems no conclusive argument why infinite complexity couldn’t be the case.

***Is the whole in some sense greater than the sum of the parts, or is it nothing more than the sum?***

That the whole possesses a property the parts do not is perfectly explicable in terms of the parts and their arrangement. (Pile of rock 1m high formed by small rocks = arrangement/combination of attributes/properties)

But for a pile of rock, length (of the pile) is a property shared by each individual rock.
But in some cases, there is no individual part which has the property enjoyed by the whole (atoms and temperature)
So it can be that the whole has a property not possessed by the parts in any degree.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Parts all the way down.md

title: Chapter 4 - What is a change?
content:
[[What's occurring?]]

[[Who can bear change?]]

[[The wholly presence]]
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Chapter 4 - What is a change?.md

title: What's occurring?
content:
Talking only about particulars and their properties seems like we are omitting the change these things undergo. It's a static description. 

A fact is an event where nothing happens: a door being brown at 12 and being brown at 20 / door having same position at two different times, etc...
These are *static* events, changeless.

In an event, however, there should be some change involved. 
Maybe even just a single change, but still some change. 

In a process a number of changes is involved, in a particular sequence. 

Some events contain other events as parts. 

A process contains a complex series of more than one change in a particular order. The order is important cos if we changed it we'd get a different process.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/What's occurring?.md

title: The more things change, the more they stay the same
content:
A particular is something other than its properties. I can imagine these properties changing while the particular remaining the same it was. 

We can say that something has changed qualitatively even though it has remained numerically the same.

Being one and the same, despite such changes in qualities, is what we mean by numerical sameness.

It could be that the particular thing itself cannot be the same thing as its properties. At least some of them could change and yet it would still be the same particular.
So when I look at and feel the properties of the particular, I am observing just that–its properties–and not the particular itself. But what, then, is the particular, if it is not its properties?

The particular is something that underlies the properties and holds them all together in one place. It is something I cannot see or touch, because all I experience is a thing’s properties, but I know it is there through my rational thinking.
The properties are clustered together in a semi-permanent way. (can change the colour of a table)

A particular that has been stripped of all its properties couldn’t look or feel like anything

Isn’t it the case that everything that exists has properties? It is not as if ‘bare’ particulars could exist and that some of them were just fortunate enough to accidentally acquire properties.

Certainly every physical thing that ever has and ever will exist has some shape or weight or feature. And to talk as if the thing can in some way exist independently of those properties was perhaps the mistake that led us to absurdity.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/The more things change, the more they stay the same.md

title: Emergentist view
content:
They say; wholes are more (or equal to) than the sum of parts. 

If we knew everything about brains and the way neurons worked, it can be claimed that even then we would not be able to predict the phenomenon of consciousness, nor what it feels like to experience something. 

A neuroscientist might know what goes on in the brain when we see red. 
But he only know WHAT IT FEELS LIKE to see red when he experience it himself. 

There are novel phenomena to be found in wholes that are not in their parts, nor their sums, nor in their arrangement.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Emergentist view.md

title: Plato's heaven
content:
Plato didn’t think you could destroy circularity. He thought that the instances of it with which we are acquainted are all imperfect copies of the true circularity.

Every circular thing existing in the physical world will be defective in its circularity, to at least some degree, no matter how small. The geometrical definition of a circle is relevant here. Plato thought that only the mathematician was properly acquainted with a perfect circle, in which every point on the circumference was exactly the same distance from the centre. But all the circles we see in the world around us will have some slight deviation from this perfect or ideal circle. Now here is the fanciful part. Plato thought that the perfect circle existed in a heavenly, transcendent world: above and beyond the physical world of everyday objects that we inhabit. This heavenly realm would contain all the true versions of all the properties and relations too.

This realm is not something we could ever see with our eyes or interact with physically. It has to be contemplated and understood through pure intellect, according to Plato.

Platonism is a very strong form of realism about properties. For Plato, they were more real than their imperfect copies that we ordinarily encounter. Only the Platonic circle was a perfect circle. All the rest are flawed. He called these perfect versions of properties ***Forms***, and he thought they were the most real things of all. We knew they existed through our intellectual grasp alone, so we didn’t have to worry about whether our senses misled us.

A Platonist divides existence into two realms: the one we inhabit and the heavenly one where properties exist.
But whenever we divide something into two realms, we have to tell a story about how they relate and that often gets complicated. That problem afflicts this account. What is the relation supposed to be between the perfect Form of a circle and the individual circles that we see around us?

One circle exists in a heavenly realm, outside space and time. The other circles all exist imperfectly in space and time. So how can they possibly relate to the Form? Can they resemble the Platonic circle when they are so different in their natures?

There is a major difficulty for any such proposal. Suppose we wanted to say something like this: the worldly circles resemble the perfect Form of a circle. Resemblance is a relation. But a relation, it will be recalled, is also something that a Platonist thinks belongs in their heavenly realm. There would be a Form of resemblance, therefore. how does the Form of resemblance relate to the actual resemblance (between the worldly circle and the Form of a circle)? 
If we give the same answer–that it resembles it–we will have made no further progress. We will have embarked on what philosophers call an infinite regress. This would indicate that the original answer is no good: we never should try to say that the instances relate to the Form.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Plato's heaven.md

title: Who can bear change?
content:
Notion of being one and the same thing in [[Chapter 1 - What is a table?]]
Knowing when one thing is the very same thing as another. When they are, we really have only one thing. 
Some say that identity is not a relation at all: when it holds, then we only have one thing, whereas all genuine relations relate at least two.

Aristotle says that change needs a subject: man with hair in 2010 and main without hair in 2020 is a change if it was one and the same man. 
This is a single-change in the class of small-scale changes. 

Some changes involve multiple subjects: energy passing from one ball to another when colliding. Is transfer of energy just one change or two separate changes?

A change can be:
- [[gain or loss of a property]], 
- [[coming into or going out of existence of something]], 
- [[change within one property]].
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Who can bear change?.md
links: gain or loss of a property
coming into or going out of existence of something

title: It's all just words
content:
The view that everything is a particular is sometimes called nominalism.

The name is applied to groups of particulars that resemble each other. 

Hence, there are particulars – a ball, a coin, a screw-top lid, a wheel, and so on – and circularity is just a name for the way in which these things resemble each other. 
Circularity itself is no thing. It has no existence or reality. Every single thing is a particular.

Problem: in what way a group of objects resemble each other?
Say in a group all are circular and brown. There is more than one way in which things of the group resemble each other. So circularity cannot just be a resemblance between the things, therefore, cos it must be one individual resemblance in a certain respect. 

So we have to refer not just to the particular things but also to *ways* in which they resemble; this sounds like a property by another name. 

Another problem: groups of particulars resembled each other, and this was all a property consisted in. But what is this resemblance thing? It sounds a relation: a universal that holds between all the particulars in the group. So we are appealing to a universal - a relation.
Then suppose that resemblance was not universal. According to nominalism, it is a particular. 
There is a particular resemblance that hold between a pair of objects. But there would also be another particular resemblance that held between a further pair of objects. 
--> *In what way are those two particular resemblances both resemblances?*
==Resemblance cannot be a real relation. So these two resemblances resemble each other. ==
And then we need an account of that further resemblance ... infinite regress.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/It's all just words.md

title: The wholly presence
content:
Not accepting temporal parts (why wouldn't you pezzo di merda)? 
How does some thing seem able to bear incompatible properties?

Aristotelian theory: a particular is said to be wholly present at every time in which it exists. 
It is not a temporal part of the tomato that is red but the whole tomato. 
This is *endurantism*, cos wholly present particulars endure through changes. How can the view explain particulars having incompatible properties? 
--> Those incompatible properties are borne at different times.
**Drawback**: properties are not held pure and simple by particulars. They've to be held relative to something else, a particular time, complicating our account of what it is to have a property.

Perdurantists ([[coming into or going out of existence of something]]) say temporal part has the property pure and simple, with no need for any further relational element to be involved. 
When something changes, we should not see it as a single thing bearing contrary properties, but as different things - temporal parts - bearing those properties. 

If a single temporal part were capable to undergo any change, then same problem would resurface. So there must be a different temporal part for every slight change. 

Subject of change enduring through change of properties: we have a succession of things with static properties, only very slightly different from the things that immediately precede and follow them. 
Change would be an illusion created by the succession of such temporal parts. 

This looks like a denial of change than a theory of change cos each temporal part is changeless, so all the kinds of change reduce to the [[coming into or going out of existence of something]]. 

A thing is a construction out of the sequence of static parts. 
So a collection of parts needs to be connected up in an appropriate way so that they make what we take to be an enduring thing. This is a matter of finding the right relations to hold between the parts. 
- Succession in time is one requirement; two distinct temporal parts that existed at the same time couldn't be a part of the same thing.
- Causation is another relation; earlier temporal parts cause the later ones to be.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/The wholly presence.md
links: coming into or going out of existence of something

title: Holism
content:
Wholes in some sense have priority over the parts. 

In contrast to [[Reductionist view]] (facts about the parts determine facts about the wholes), evoke evolutionary selection in which facts about wholes seem to be determining facts about the parts.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Holism.md

title: A symmetrical universe
content:
What we just had was a proposed first way for the bundle theorist to avoid the implication that particulars with all the same properties collapse into one. As that didn’t seem to work, here is a second proposal. The objection, that the theory entails bundles with all the same properties must be one and the same, strikes only if the properties are to be understood in a certain way: as nothing like particulars.

Perhaps those properties are particularized in some way. Hence, the red in this bundle might be a different thing or instance from the red in another bundle. There might then be the possibility that there are distinct particulars with all the same properties. They consist of the same types of property but different instances of them. Isn’t this what we think of all the red snooker balls? The red of this ball is not the same as the red of that ball. They are two different instances of red.

But there is again a problem with this apparent solution. We have saved the bundle theory but at a cost. An advantage of the bundle theory, it was noted, was that it accounted for particulars entirely in terms of properties. Particularity was reduced away in terms of properties. But it now seems that we are able to salvage the bundle theory from the objection that two identical bundles would collapse into one only if we understand properties in some way as particulars. We spoke of having two distinct instances of red and a property instance looks like some kind of particular. So to make our bundles behave more like the particulars that we take objects to be, we have had to make our properties like particulars. Particularity has managed to sneak back into the theory.

But it looks like we might be forced to conclude that particularity is an irreducible feature of reality, for there could, in theory, be two distinct particulars whose distinctness did not consist in them having different properties.

So what, then, is a table? After the considerations in this chapter, it seems that we have to say it is a particular that bears certain properties but is not identical with, nor reducible to, those properties.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/A symmetrical universe.md

title: Identical twins
content:
According to bundle theory, a particular is just a collection of properties. It tells us that a particular just is the bundle. But then, if we have the same bundle, it implies that we have the same object. In other words, there could not, on this theory, be more than one object that is the same bundle of properties.

Couldn’t it just be that, as a matter of fact, two distinct objects never really do share all the same properties?
This response misses the point of a philosophical theory, however. This was supposed to be an account of what it is to be a particular thing. The truth of that theory should not have to rely on luck working out for it, such that every particular thing just happens to be a different bundle. It does seem at least a possibility that two things could share all their properties. And if, as the theory states, particulars are only and nothing more than bundles of properties, then it is inconsistent with that possibility. Two particulars with the same properties collapse into one.

The first apparent solution is to say that there is a reason in principle why two particulars could not share all their properties. If one allows relational properties, then these arguably must differ because they allow spatiotemporal location to come into the equation.

Here is the problem with this proposal. There is no guarantee that distinct things really will have different relational properties unless we reintroduce particulars into our metaphysics. This is why. Should we think of position in space (and time) as an absolute or relative matter? If it were absolute, it would suggest that there is some kind of particularity to spatial positions. A position would be a particular. The notion of a particular–one that is not defined as a bundle of qualities–will have come back into the theory. That’s no good because we were looking to eliminate particulars in terms of bundles of properties.

So do we instead define spatial positions in relation to each other? The problem with doing so is that there is at least the possibility that the space of a universe has a line of symmetry; and thus places in corresponding positions either side of the line of symmetry would bear an identical set of relations to all the other places within the whole of that space. If we then position two of our snooker balls at those corresponding points within our symmetrical universe, then it remains a theoretical possibility that two distinct particulars nevertheless are identical in all their non-relational and relational properties. On the bundle theory, they again collapse into each other.
![[Pasted image 20240620144247.png]]

##### Summary
We tried to separate indistinguishable particulars on the basis of them having different locations. But either those locations are themselves particulars, in which case we have not succeeded in eliminating particulars, or locations are just distinguished by their relations to each other. And in the latter case, the possibility of a symmetrical structure means that we could have two particulars that were not distinguishable even on the basis of location.

[[A symmetrical universe]]
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Identical twins.md

title: Related matters
content:
it should be noted that a distinction is usually made between properties and relations. 
it is arguable that this relation of being taller than is wholly present in each of its instances. Perhaps even more so than in the case of properties, it seems clear that it is the very same thing that appears in both the case of Alan being taller than Bobby and Bobby being taller than Clarissa. When we say one thing is taller than another, it seems that in every case we mean the same thing.

Suppose someone were to gather every circular thing in the world, or every red thing, or every one- hundred-sided thing. No doubt this is a practical impossibility, but it is worth entertaining just as a thought experiment. Suppose they were then to crush all the circular things, or whatever the example is, until they were destroyed or at least no longer circular. Would they thereby have destroyed circularity? Arguably not. At best, they have destroyed all the instances of it. But can we say that circularity nevertheless exists? If so, where or when does it exist?
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Related matters.md

title: Chapter 1 - What is a table?
content:
Tables, chairs, buildings are particular things. 
But we really cannot avoid mentioning properties as soon as we mention the particulars to which they attach.

[[The more things change, the more they stay the same]]

[[Bundles of properties]]

[[Identical twins]]
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Chapter 1 - What is a table?.md
links: The more things change, the more they stay the same

title: Back down to earth
content:
Plato’s theory was described as a realism about properties but it is not the only form that such realism could take. The worry about it concerned its transcendent nature: properties residing in the Platonic heaven. But perhaps the properties could be real and exist down here, in the regular world of which we feel part. Such was Aristotle’s view, which we can call an immanent realism, because properties are here with us. Circularity would be a real feature of the world but exist only in its instances: in circular things.

One would have to accept that some such circles were imperfect. Perhaps that means that only imperfect circularity is a real property. Why not accept that? The mathematician’s circle is really nothing more than a stipulated definition of something, which doesn’t mean that it thereby exists. To exist, on this view, is for something to be it; and if nothing is perfectly circular, mathematically defined, then perfect circularity is not a property of our world.

But what of the point that if I crush every circular object, I cannot thereby have destroyed circularity? It doesn’t seem that properties can go out of existence or come into it. Although this point may tempt one towards Platonism, there is another response. 
Suppose I say that a property exists in nothing more than its instances, but I mean in all the instances that ever have and ever will be? We can treat all times as equal rather than privileging the present. So if something somewhere is circular, be it only once, at any time, then that property exists and is real.
file_path: Philosophy/Intro to Metaphysics/files/Back down to earth.md

title: Going round in circles
content:
We can also speak of a circle, as if it is a particular thing. But circularity has a very different kind of nature. Unlike the case of the pen, the fact that circularity appears in one place or time does not stop it appearing at other places and times. And at all those places it appears, it is wholly present.

This is different from the case of the pen. It being wholly owned by one person stops it being owned by another, whereas one thing being circular does not stop other things being circular. And when the pen is co-owned, each of the owners owns only part of the pen. But if a number of things are circular, each is wholly circular. It is not as if circularity has to be shared out in portions, such that if exactly two things had it, for instance, each would be semi-circular. That would be absurd because then neither of those things would be circular after all.

This is what is meant by speaking of circularity being wholly present in all of its instances and it being a One thing that runs through Many others.

One view is that there are two basic kinds of entity: particulars and their properties. Tables, chairs, and sheep are examples of particulars and they seem present only at one location at a time. Circularity is an example of a property: a feature or quality of a particular. The fact that a property appears in one place, in its entirety, puts no limit on it appearing elsewhere at other places and times. Because of this feature, some like to call properties universals–they can be at any place or time–though really this term is best used for one particular theory of what a property is.

title: Syllogism
content:
A syllogism is a type of reasoning where a conclusion can be deduced based on a series of specific premises or assumptions. 

### Structure of a Syllogism:

1. **Major Premise**:
    - A general statement or universal truth.
    - Example: "All men are mortal."

2. **Minor Premise**:    
    - A specific statement related to the major premise.
    - Example: "Socrates is a man."

3. **Conclusion**:    
    - A statement that logically follows from the two premises.
    - Example: "Therefore, Socrates is mortal."

### Types of Syllogisms:

1. **Categorical Syllogism**:
    - Involves statements that begin with "all," "no," or "some."
    - Example:
        - Major Premise: "All birds have wings."
        - Minor Premise: "A sparrow is a bird."
        - Conclusion: "Therefore, a sparrow has wings."

2. **Conditional (Hypothetical) Syllogism**:    
    - Involves "if... then..." statements.
    - Example:
        - Major Premise: "If it rains, the ground will be wet."
        - Minor Premise: "It is raining."
        - Conclusion: "Therefore, the ground is wet."

3. **Disjunctive Syllogism**:    
    - Involves "either... or..." statements.
    - Example:
        - Major Premise: "Either it is raining, or it is sunny."
        - Minor Premise: "It is not raining."
        - Conclusion: "Therefore, it is sunny."

title: Plato's cave
content:
Human perception exists without anyone being aware of the existence of Forms, and how true knowledge is only gained through philosophy. 

Knowledge gained thru senses is opinion, NOT knowledge. Only thru philosophical reasoning that one is able to pursue knowledge.

Prisoners in a cave see only shadows cast on the wall, mistaking them for reality. One prisoner escapes and discovers the outside world, realizing the shadows are mere imitations of real objects.

#### Meaning:
People mistake the appearance of what is in front of them as reality and live in ignorance. When parts of the truth start to emerge, it can be frightening and can make people want to turn back. If one does not turn away from the truth and continues to see it, he will have a better understanding of the world around him. 
Freed prisoner is the philosopher seeking a greater truth outside of the perceived reality.

By the time they go back, every piece of wood has been replaced. 

Q: was the ship that they returned on the same ship that they left on, even tho it was made of completely different pieces of wood?

title: The Science of Making and Breaking Habits
content:
## What a Habit Is

- A **habit** is a behavior your nervous system has learned (often without conscious effort).
    
- A large share of daily behavior is habitual, so changing habits is largely about changing “default” actions.
    
- Habit change is a form of **neuroplasticity**: the brain rewires based on repeated experience, building circuits that make some behaviors more likely and others less likely.
    

## Two Types of Habits to Build

- **Goal-based habits**
    
    - Focus on a specific outcome each time (e.g., “do Zone 2 cardio 4x/week”).
        
    - Easy to track and “check off.”
        
- **Identity-based habits**
    
    - Tie the habit to who you are becoming (e.g., “I’m a fit person,” “I’m an athlete”).
        
    - Often more motivating long-term because the habit supports a bigger self-image.
        

## How Long Habit Formation Takes

- There is no universal “21 days.”
    
- Evidence shows habit formation can take **18 to 254 days**, depending on the person and the habit.
    
- Takeaway: slow progress doesn’t mean you’re “bad at habits”—you may just have higher friction for that specific habit.
    

## **Limbic Friction** (The Hidden Reason Habits Feel Hard)

- **Limbic friction = the activation energy** required to start a behavior.
    
- It often comes from two opposite states:
    
    - **Too anxious/over-activated** (can’t settle into the task).
        
    - **Too tired/unmotivated** (can’t “get going”).
        
- Practical use: treat habit difficulty as a **state problem**, not a “willpower problem.”
    

## **Linchpin Habits** (The “One Habit That Pulls Others With It”)

- **Linchpin habits** are habits you **enjoy** that make many other good habits easier.
    
- Example pattern:
    
    - Enjoyable exercise → better sleep choices → better food choices → better focus → more consistency overall.
        
- Key rule: a lynchpin habit is usually **something you like doing**, so it’s easy to repeat—and it “tilts” your day toward better behaviors.
    

## What Makes a Habit “Strong”

A habit is stronger when:

- **Context-independent**: you do it regardless of location/schedule changes (travel, weekends, chaos).
    
- **Low limbic friction**: it takes little mental effort to start.
    
- The end goal is **automaticity**: the behavior runs with minimal conscious push.
    

## Tool 1: **Mentally Rehearse the Steps** (Procedural Review)

- Before starting a new habit, do a quick run-through of the exact sequence:
    
    - “Walk to kitchen → turn on machine → make espresso → drink.”
        
- Why it works: mentally stepping through activates circuits similar to doing the behavior, lowering the start threshold later.
    
- Action:
    
    - Do **one short visualization** of the full process (optionally repeat once).
        

## Tool 2: **Task Bracketing** (Make the Brain “Bookend” the Habit)

- The brain has habit-related circuits (basal ganglia) that fire strongly at:
    
    - **The beginning of the habit**
        
    - **The end of the habit**
        
- This “bracketing” helps the brain tag the behavior as a repeatable unit, increasing consistency even on bad days.
    
- Practical implication: create a consistent **start cue** and **end cue** so the behavior becomes easier to launch and finish.
    

## A Better Way to Schedule Habits: Use **Phases**, Not Exact Times

The brain tends to run habits based on **state**, not clock time. So instead of “7:00 AM sharp,” anchor habits to phases after waking:

### Phase 1: **0–8 hours after waking** (Best for hard habits)

- Brain chemistry is more action/focus oriented (higher drive-related neuromodulators).
    
- Put your **highest limbic friction** habits here (the ones you resist most).
    
- Examples:
    
    - Hard training, deep work, difficult studying, intimidating admin tasks.
        

### Phase 2: **~9–14/15 hours after waking** (Best for “mellow” habits)

- The body trends toward a calmer state; good for habits requiring less friction.
    
- Good fits:
    
    - Journaling, language practice, light creative work, easier learning tasks.
        
- Helpful supports in this phase:
    
    - **Taper bright artificial light** later in the day (sunlight is fine).
        
    - Relaxation-oriented behaviors (e.g., heat/sauna/hot shower) can support wind-down.
        

### Phase 3: **~16–24 hours after waking** (Protect sleep = protect habit learning)

- Sleep is when the brain “saves” the rewiring you triggered during the day.
    
- Priorities:
    
    - **Dim lights at night** (especially if you wake up—use minimal light to avoid suppressing melatonin).
        
    - Keep the sleep environment **cool and dark/dim**.
        
    - Avoid turning nights into high-stress, high-stimulation periods.
        
- Core idea: if sleep is disrupted, habit wiring becomes much harder.
    

## Once a Habit Is Built: Make It Flexible

- After the habit becomes easy, **moving it around** (different times/contexts) can strengthen it further.
    
- This builds **context independence**—proof the habit is truly embedded.
    

## A Structured System: The **21-Day Habit Program**

- For 21 days:
    
    - Write **6 habits** you aim to do each day.
        
    - Expect to complete **4–5** most days (built-in realism).
        
- Two important rules:
    
    - **No punishment** for a miss.
        
    - **No “slip compensation”** (don’t do 8 the next day to make up for 4 yesterday).
        
- After 21 days:
    
    - Stop forcing it and assess: which habits became **automatic**?
        
- Repeat cycles only when:
    
    - The current set is truly becoming reflexive, or you clearly see what didn’t stick and why.
        

## How to Break a Bad Habit (Without Relying on Perfect Self-Control)

- Many bad habits happen too fast to interrupt beforehand.
    
- Strategy: immediately **after** the bad habit happens, insert a **replacement behavior**.
    
- Why this works:
    
    - The bad-habit circuits were just active, so the brain is primed for rewiring.
        
    - You create a new linked pattern: **bad habit → good follow-up habit**.
        
- Rules for the replacement behavior:
    
    - It must be **positive** and **easy to execute** (low friction).
        
    - The goal is not “rewarding” the bad habit—it’s **rewriting the sequence** so the brain learns a new default ending.
        

## Golden Nuggets

- **Habit difficulty often reflects limbic friction (state), not character.**
    
- **Use Phase 1 for hard habits, Phase 2 for mellow habits, Phase 3 to protect sleep-based rewiring.**
    
- **Task bracketing (strong start + strong finish) is a major driver of consistency.**
    
- **To break habits, don’t fight the moment before—change what happens immediately after.**

title: Journaling - Huberman Lab notes
content:
Write down deepest emotions and thoughts as they relate to the most upsetting experience in your life. How has this experience related to who you have become? Who you have been in the past and who you will want to become in the future?

In 15-30 min
5/10 min to transition back to re enter daily living (so not in the evening)

Do not stop writing, do not pause, do not care about form and grammar. Just write instinctively what you feel like writing about it.
Do not read what you’ve written.

Do it 4 days in a row or 1 day a week for 4 weeks

After the 4th session wait a week and then go over what you wrote, in each session. 
Careful to the number of words used reflecting negative emotions or states: compare the 1st to the 2nd to the 3rd to the 4th entry.
Do the same for the positive states that emerge from your writing.

Trauma is any experience that modify our brain such that we do not function as well emotionally behaviourally and cognitively going forward. 

Think about 3 things before start writing:
- Facts about that difficult experience
- Emotions that were felt at the time and emotions that u feel now about that experience
- Include links that come to mind about negative experience and anything that’s happening now, in the past, or in the future, that you think may be related to the experience. Whatever might link to that experience. 

Try writing in full sentences. Do not care about words used and the form, just write without caring.

title: Maximising productivity - Huberman Lab notes
content:
When wake up write down time of waking up.
The lowest temperature of our body during the day is on average 2h before the wake up time.

Taking a walk after waking up is good. It’s a motion of forward ambulation.

Don’t protect yourself from blue light early in the day. Turn off screen red lights. 

The state of being fasted refer to the insulin level of your body and level of blood sugar. 

If you do a lot of physical exercise early in the morning you can eat relatively much since your insuline level is low. 

Should find a way to ingest caffeine without drinking coffee, 90-120 min after wake up.  
Yerba mate.  

Drinking lot of water with pinch of sea salt.

Try keep your screens at eye levels if not higher to increase alertness.

Body posture influences alertness. 

Follow the ultradian cycles (90min) 

Shape it with pomodoro format.

Avere la vescica piena aumenta agitazione che aumenta alertness of mind. So drink and keep it inside for whatever reasonable amount of time not to suffer but also not to pee every 15min.

Discomfort must not lead you to be distracted. 

Freedom app or website to shut down from distractions.

These cycles of deep work must be intense. No distractions. No checking whatever. Just focus and work. Read in the pauses and if tired just relax doing nothing or stare at a point to regain focus.

Best quality work is usually done after 4-6 hours the temperature minimum. Try place the 90/120min deep focused work in this time span. 

Say you wake up at 6. Lowest temperature was around 4. 4-6h after is 8-10. Try fit a deep focused work session in this time range. 

After the lowest temperature, body temperature will start rising all throughout the morning and afternoon. To optimise this 2h focused session try catch the steepest slope of temperature rise.
Quindi è bene che questa sessione sia nel periodo in cui la tua temperatura aumenta nel modo più veloce della giornata. 

After this session of focused work you should do physical exercise for around 60min(+-15min). 

To improve cardiovascular health best is 5 days per week of physical practice.

Ingesting electrolytes early in the morning for focus.
Ingesting 1000mg of epa oils
Or omega 3 fatty acids food

Walk after lunch 5-30min for metabolism.

Nsdr practices after lunch to restore energy levels

Seeing sunlight as the Sun goes down is beneficial because it makes your retina less sensitive to the light you will see in the later part of the afternoon and evening, thus you will be less sensitive to light prior to sleep. 
Still, you will need to dim bright light in the evening .

Starchy carbs food for dinner for inducing sleep. (Non refined sugar)  

Hot shower in the evening induces sleep.

Keep the room cool.  

One day (maybe half a day) per week you should get full rest to recover properly meaning don’t do things in a structured way. Just relax do what you like do what makes you happy do your hobbies see friends

title: Paul Conti Mental Health - Huberman Lab
content:
Agency, often referred to as personal autonomy or self-determination, signifies the capacity to make choices and decisions that reflect our own values and desires. It is the sense that we are in control of our lives and possess the power to shape our destinies. Agency is an integral facet of mental health, empowering individuals to navigate challenges, set and attain goals, and cultivate a robust sense of self.

Gratitude is a positive and appreciative attitude towards life and the world around us. It involves recognising and acknowledging the goodness, no matter how small, that exists in our lives. Gratitude is not merely a pleasant feeling; it is a potent tool for enhancing mental health and overall well-being.

[The Cornerstones of Mental Health: Agency and Gratitude | by Kulwant Saluja | Medium](https://medium.com/@kulwantsaluja/the-cornerstones-of-mental-health-agency-and-gratitude-28d64dc4a7d6)

title: Nature & Focus - Huberman Lab
content:
###  Key ideas mapped

1. **Attention Restoration Theory (ART)** – Nature provides “softly fascinating” stimuli that engage involuntary attention, allowing directed‑attention resources to recover.
    
2. **Fractals & complexity matching** – Repeating natural patterns resonate with the visual cortex, lowering cognitive load and stress while boosting focus.
    
3. **Dose matters** – Even **10–15 min** of outdoor greenery or looking at high‑resolution nature images can measurably improve working‑memory span and decrease heart‑rate variability.
    
4. **Nature vs. digital environments** – Social‑media scrolling exerts the opposite attentional pull, fragmenting focus and shortening time perspective.
    
5. **Green‑space equity & public health** – Tree‑canopy density in urban neighborhoods predicts lower cardiovascular‑disease rates independent of income.
    
6. **Design implications** – Biophilic architecture, interior plants, water features and natural soundscapes replicate many of the restorative effects indoors.
    
7. **Child‑specific protocols** – “Nature free‑play” (unstructured exploration) increases executive‑function scores and reduces screen‑induced hyper‑stimulation.
    
8. **Comparative tools** – Meditation improves top‑down control; nature restores bottom‑up resources. They are complementary, not redundant.
    
9. **Practical blueprint** – Schedule a brief, phone‑free outdoor walk before cognitively demanding work; intersperse 5‑min “gaze out the window at trees” micro‑breaks every 90 min; bring fractal artwork or a desktop plant into your workspace.

### Most important take‑aways

1. **Nature is an attentional “battery charger.”** Ten minutes in greenery or even 40 seconds of fractal imagery measurably restore the neural circuits of focus.
    
2. **Combine, don’t replace.** Meditation builds attention control; nature replenishes it. Use both strategically.
    
3. **Green your workspace.** A single plant, a window view, or looping natural‑sound audio gives ~20 % of the benefit of going outside.
    
4. **Pre‑task exposure > post‑task.** A brief nature break _before_ deep‑work blocks increases sustained attention more than a break afterward.
    
5. **Kids need unstructured “green play.”** It counteracts smartphone‑induced attentional narrowing and supports executive‑function development.
    
6. **Health ripple‑effects.** More urban trees correlate with lower heart‑disease incidence; mechanisms include air‑quality, physical‑activity cues and stress buffering.
    
7. **Design matters.** Incorporating fractals, natural textures, variable lighting and indirect sounds of water can make classrooms, hospitals and offices cognitively restorative.
    
8. **Digital overload = “mental obesity.”** Continuous passive screen use taxes attention similarly to junk food’s effect on metabolism; nature is a proven antidote.
    
9. **Equity imperative.** Access to restorative environments is a public‑health need, not a luxury; urban‑planning policies should reflect that.

## Foundational Concepts in Attention and Mental Fatigue

## Directed Versus Involuntary Attention

Dr. Berman introduces a crucial distinction between two types of human attention that forms the cornerstone of attention restoration theory. Directed attention represents the effortful, conscious decision-making process where individuals choose what to focus on, such as listening to a lecture or reading a complex document. This type of attention is uniquely human in its sophistication and allows for sustained focus on tasks that may not be inherently interesting but are necessary for goal achievement.

In contrast, involuntary attention operates automatically, captured by inherently interesting or stimulating elements in the environment such as bright lights, sudden movements, or beautiful natural scenes. This type of attention requires no conscious effort and appears to be less susceptible to fatigue compared to directed attention. The distinction is critical because it explains why we can watch a captivating movie for hours without feeling mentally drained, yet struggle to maintain focus during a lengthy work session.

## The Modern Attention Crisis

The contemporary human experience involves unprecedented demands on directed attention, creating what Dr. Berman describes as a directed attention fatigue epidemic. Unlike our ancestors who faced fewer competing stimuli, modern individuals must constantly filter and prioritize information from multiple sources simultaneously. This creates a depletion of attentional resources that manifests as reduced impulse control, increased aggression, and difficulty achieving personal goals.

The physiological basis of this fatigue remains partially understood, though Dr. Berman notes the puzzle that brain metabolism remains relatively constant at approximately 20% of total body metabolism regardless of mental activity level. This suggests that attention fatigue may involve qualitative changes in neural processing rather than simple energy depletion, pointing to more complex mechanisms involving neurotransmitter systems and neural network efficiency.

## Nature's Impact on Cognitive Function

## Attention Restoration Theory in Practice

The core premise of attention restoration theory is that natural environments can restore depleted directed attention by gently engaging involuntary attention. This restoration occurs through what Dr. Berman describes as "softly fascinating stimulation" - natural elements that capture attention without demanding effortful processing. Examples include the movement of leaves in wind, the sound of flowing water, or the complex yet harmonious patterns found throughout nature.

This restoration process appears to be remarkably efficient, with even brief periods of nature exposure producing measurable improvements in cognitive performance. The benefits extend beyond the immediate experience, with research showing enhanced attentional capacity persisting for hours or even days after nature exposure. This suggests that nature interactions create lasting changes in neural function rather than providing temporary relief.

## Fractals and Natural Patterns

Dr. Berman discusses the unique mathematical properties of natural environments, particularly the prevalence of fractal patterns that appear at multiple scales throughout nature. These self-similar patterns, found in everything from tree branches to coastlines, seem to be processed by the human visual system in ways that promote relaxation and attention restoration. The fractal geometry of nature may have evolutionary significance, as humans developed in environments rich with these patterns.

The discussion of fractals extends to their potential applications in design and architecture, suggesting that incorporating fractal-like patterns into built environments could provide some of the cognitive benefits associated with nature exposure. This represents a practical bridge between understanding natural pattern recognition and creating more restorative indoor spaces.

## Comparative Environmental Analysis

## Urban Versus Natural Environments

The research presents compelling evidence for differential effects of urban and natural environments on brain function. Urban environments, characterized by straight lines, sharp angles, and high contrast elements, tend to demand directed attention and can contribute to mental fatigue. The complexity and unpredictability of urban stimuli require constant vigilance and decision-making, depleting cognitive resources over time.

Natural environments demonstrate the opposite effect, with their organic curves, varied textures, and harmonious color palettes promoting relaxation and attention restoration. The semantic content of natural scenes also differs significantly from urban environments, with nature images typically evoking positive emotions and memories while urban scenes may trigger stress responses associated with navigation challenges or safety concerns.

## Image Compression and Cognitive Processing

An interesting aspect of the discussion involves the relationship between image complexity and cognitive processing demands. Natural scenes, despite their apparent complexity, often compress more efficiently in digital formats due to their harmonic color relationships and organic patterns. This compression efficiency may reflect how the human visual system processes natural versus artificial environments, with natural scenes requiring less cognitive effort to interpret and understand.

This technical insight has practical implications for environmental design, suggesting that spaces designed with natural color harmonies and organic patterns may be less cognitively demanding than those featuring high contrast artificial elements. The compression analogy provides a useful framework for understanding why natural environments feel more restful despite containing significant visual information.

## Health Benefits and Practical Applications

## Physical Health Improvements

Beyond cognitive benefits, nature exposure demonstrates measurable improvements in physical health markers. The discussion covers evidence for reduced stress hormone levels, improved immune function, and better cardiovascular health associated with regular nature contact. These benefits appear to occur through multiple pathways, including reduced stress response, increased physical activity, and exposure to beneficial microorganisms found in natural environments.

The concept of "forest bathing" or shinrin-yoku receives attention as a structured approach to nature exposure that maximizes health benefits. This practice involves mindful, immersive experiences in forest environments that go beyond simple outdoor recreation to create therapeutic encounters with nature.

## Biophilic Design and Indoor Applications

For individuals with limited access to natural environments, Dr. Berman discusses biophilic design principles that bring natural elements indoors. These applications range from simple interventions like adding plants or natural images to more comprehensive design approaches that incorporate natural materials, lighting patterns, and airflow systems that mimic outdoor environments.

The effectiveness of these interventions varies, with live plants and natural materials generally providing greater benefits than artificial alternatives. However, even nature photography and recorded natural sounds can provide measurable cognitive benefits, making these interventions accessible to virtually anyone regardless of living situation or geographic location.

## Specific Strategies and Recommendations

## Optimal Duration and Timing

The research reveals that nature benefits follow a dose-response relationship, but with diminishing returns beyond certain exposure levels. Brief nature breaks of 5-10 minutes can provide noticeable attention restoration, while longer exposures of 20-50 minutes appear to maximize cognitive benefits. The timing of nature exposure also matters, with pre-work nature breaks showing particular effectiveness for enhancing subsequent focused work performance.

For individuals in urban environments, the recommendation includes incorporating multiple brief nature contacts throughout the day rather than relying on occasional longer exposures. This approach acknowledges the rapid depletion of attentional resources in demanding environments and provides regular restoration opportunities.

## Technology Integration and Limitations

The discussion addresses the complex relationship between technology use and attention depletion. While technology itself is not inherently problematic, the way it is commonly used - with constant notifications, rapid task switching, and high visual contrast - contributes significantly to directed attention fatigue. The recommendation involves mindful technology use combined with regular nature-based restoration periods.

Social media receives particular attention as a form of "passive depleting activity" that drains cognitive resources without providing restoration. This contrasts with what Dr. Berman terms "passive restorative activities," which include nature observation, listening to natural sounds, or viewing natural scenes that restore rather than deplete attentional capacity.

title: Focus - Huberman Lab notes
content:
Always keep into account 5 to 10 minutes to enter proper focus mode.

When doing 15 minutes break at the end of a two hour cycle, do not read or make demanding intellectual activities, just take a break and let your brain decompose into rest mode 

The intensity of the work or skills relates directly to the amount of rest one needs to take after practising at the skill. So if you are able to focus very sharply and very quickly for prolonged amount of times, then you might need more time to rest and decompose your mind.

Cold exposure increases concentration.

Coming from a state of non-focus back to focus again is a major driver of neuroplasticity 

Before starting a session of deep concentration , so, for example full cycle of Pomodoro, take from 30 seconds to 3 minutes and focus your vision on a particular and narrow spot.

If you feel like sometimes that your focus has faded down, then take another break of a couple of minutes to do the exercise again, and focus your attention on a narrow spot of your vision.

The object of your vision and attention must be different from the task that you have been trying to tackle for the focus session.

5G a day of creatine monohydrate

title: Use Failure for Learning - Huberman lab
content:
The way to create plasticity is to create mismatches or errors in how we perform things. The way is to send signals to the brain that something is wrong, different, isn’t being achieved.

Flow is not a state for learning, is an expression to do what we know how to do.

The brain changes when certain chemicals (acido choline, epinephrine, dopamine) are released in ways and time that allows for neuro circuits to reside, that take place during sleep

As an adult do shorter bouts of learning with less content to learn

How badly we want or need the plasticity determines how fast the plasticity will arrive

Attach dopamine to failures and mistakes during bouts of not too long work. During these sessions seek errors and repetition of mistakes, as seeking the frustration given by them. Optimal would be finding pleasure in the frustration.

Reducing frustration can be done thru double sigh.

Visualizing failure routinely increases chances of success.

title: Memory - Huberman lab
content:
Sheer repetition is sufficient to learn

Hebb’s postulate: if a sequence of neurons is active at the same time, that would lead to a strengthening of the connections between this neurons 

Neurons are not themselves smart, don’t have knowledge

Most of the times, when we learn something, it’s because existing neurons strengthen their connection through coactivation through repetition over and over, OR
Through very strong activation once and only once

We remember through repetition and intensity

If one evokes the release of adrenaline, arms into ice water, the information read previously, even just a minute before, is retained as effectively as emotional information, so information retained through the linking of strong, emotional experience.

It is the emotionality evoked by an experience or emotional state you are in after you experience smth that dictates whether or not you will learn it quickly

The best time to evoke, these spike of adrenaline is either immediately after the repetition of the material that has to be learned or a couple of minutes, Few minutes after the repetition of the material to be learned

Immediately after the repeating task is important to evoke, high states of adrenaline. After sometime as one hour it might be beneficial. Also to have a nap of 20/30 minutes to enhance learning and help the retaining of the information.

One way to evoke adrenaline after The repetition task is by taking a cold shower immediately after the task. 
Or, might drink a coffee immediately after the repeating task.

Physical exercise 1-3 hours before the learning task helps the cognitive task.

Exercise before or after learning session makes a lot of sense. Exercising after learning session makes a lot of sense, especially if the exercise stimulates a lot of Adrenaline.

title: The Science of Building Strong Social Bonds
content:
## The Core Concept: "Social Homeostasis"

Huberman explains that our brains have a built-in "social gauge," similar to how we feel hunger or thirst. This system, called social homeostasis, constantly measures whether we are getting enough (or too much) social connection.
 * The "Social Hunger" Circuit: A specific part of the brain (the dorsal raphe nucleus) activates when we are lonely, creating a craving for social contact just like we crave food when fasting.
 * Why it matters: This proves that social connection is not just a "nice to have"—it is a biological need. Fulfilling it regulates our stress levels and overall health.

## Introverts vs. Extroverts (The Biology)
Whether you are an introvert or an extrovert depends largely on how your brain handles dopamine (the chemical of motivation and reward) during social interactions.
 * Introverts: They are highly sensitive to dopamine. A little bit of social interaction releases enough dopamine to feel satisfied quickly. Too much interaction can feel overwhelming or exhausting because their "reward bucket" fills up fast.
 * Extroverts: They release less dopamine per interaction or have a higher threshold to feel satisfied. They need more interaction and stimulation to fill their "reward bucket," which is why they gain energy from being around large groups.
 * Key Takeaway: Neither is "better." It is simply a difference in how much social interaction is required to feel balanced.
 
## The "Holy Trinity" of Social Chemicals
Three main chemicals drive our relationships:
 * Dopamine: The driver of motivation. It makes us seek out others and feel excitement about new connections.
 * Oxytocin: The chemical of bonding. It helps cement long-term attachments and trust (often released during touch or deep conversation).
 * Serotonin: The chemical of satisfaction. It gives us the feeling of safety, calm, and "having enough" after a good interaction.

## Actionable Tools for Better Bonds
Huberman shares science-based tools to strengthen connections with friends, family, and partners:
 * 1. Share Experiences (Physiological Synchrony):
   The fastest way to bond is to experience the same emotion or physiological state at the same time.
   * What to do: Do an activity that raises your heart rates together (like a workout or watching a scary movie) or calms you down together (like a walk in nature). When your bodies are "synced up," your brains bond faster.
 * 2. The Power of "Gaze" and "Voice":
   Eye contact and the tone of voice are primal signals of safety.
   * What to do: Engaging in deliberate eye contact (without staring weirdly) and using a warm, melodic tone of voice triggers the release of oxytocin in the other person, making them feel safer and more connected to you.
 * 3. Cognitive & Emotional Empathy:
   True bonding requires two types of empathy:
   * Emotional Empathy: "I feel what you feel" (e.g., feeling sad when they are sad).
   * Cognitive Empathy: "I understand how you see the world" (understanding their perspective even if you don't feel the emotion).
   * Tip: Great relationships actively practice both.

## Summary Takeaway
We are biologically wired to connect. Loneliness is simply a biological alarm bell (like hunger) telling us to seek people out. By understanding whether you need more or less stimulation (introvert vs. extrovert) and using shared activities to "sync" your nervous systems, you can build deeper, healthier relationships.
book: The Science of Building Strong Social Bonds.md

title: Growth mindset - Huberman lab
content:
A mindset is a mental frame that selectively organises and encodes information

Ask yourself these questions:
- What have I been told I’m really good at?
- What have I been told I’m really poor at?
- What have I told myself I’m really good at?
- What have I told myself I’m really bad at?
- What am I good at and why? Did it come naturally to me? Did I apply myself for many years? 
- Why am I not good at other things? Because you’ve never applied into those things? Or because you tried and had early failure? Or tried and tried and kept on failing?
- To what extent your label/identity is attached to the things you’re good at and bad at?

Effort based feedback is better than label based feedback 

- What is your narrative when you are engaging in things you believe you are good at?
- What is your narrative (internal dialogue) when you’re engaging in things you’re not good at? Or when you think about engaging in these things

Attach ONLY effort to the feedback of a result: whether good or bad doesn’t matter just highlight and stick to the effort you committed. Do not label you as good or bad or anything, labels of identity do counter your objectivity when you fail in what you’re good at. Simply assess your commitment 

Learning that stress can improve your performance, improves your performance.

This is the stress-is-enhancing mindset.

Turn on artificial light as bright as it can be. Then when sunrise happens go out and take sunlight anyway

title: Non dovresti sentirti a tuo agio mentre cresci
content:
Nella zona di paura, lontano dalla zona familiare, prevalgono le scuse, mancanza di fiducia in se stessi, paura di essere giudicati dagli altri. 

Qui le scuse sono la nostra prima linea di difesa contro l’ignoto, sono I dubbi e I pensieri che cercano di mantenerci al sicuro, ma che in realtà ci tengono bloccati

  

Nella zona di crescita riesci a trovare un vero, scopo, vivere I tuoi sogni, …

Stai realizzando il tuo potenziale.

È il punto in cui il disagio si trasforma in consapevolezza. 

  

Il disagio non è un segnale che stai sbagliando, ma che stai crescendo. 

  

Come un muscolo che cresce solo sotto sforzo, la nostra mente e spirito crescono solo se “stressati” in modo positivo per espandersi. 

La vulnerabilità che senti è la porta verso nuove esperienze e verso una versione più forte e consapevole di te.

  

Chi cresce veramente è chi sopporta questo disagio. 

Chi sta nell’incertezza, sente la paura, e continua ad andare avanti, nonostante tutto.

title: How to be ambitious without burning out
content:
## Core Theme

- Ambition is praised, but it often comes with **burnout**, **health costs**, and a constant feeling of “never enough.”
    
- The goal is not “be less ambitious,” but to build a **healthy relationship with ambition** that supports your life instead of consuming it.
    

## What the “Ambition Trap” Is

- The **Ambition Trap** = a double bind:
    
    - **Systemic pressure:** Some groups (e.g., women, people of color, queer people, people with disabilities) face an **ambition penalty**—they’re judged or punished for being visibly ambitious.
        
    - **Internalized pressure:** Those external messages get absorbed and turned inward (“Who am I to take up space?”), leading to self-doubt and self-sabotage.
        
- Result: You want to grow and be seen, but you also fear the consequences of being seen.
    

## Why Modern “Success Culture” Makes It Worse

- Society sells ambition as **“more for more’s sake”**:
    
    - more money, more status, more productivity, more achievements, always.
        
- This “always stretching” mindset can cost:
    
    - **health**, **relationships**, and **joy**
        
    - and can reinforce unhealthy or oppressive systems by rewarding exhaustion and overwork.
        

## A Better Model: **Ambition Comes in Cycles**

- Ambition isn’t meant to be constant. It should move like **seasons**:
    
    - growth → peak → rest → renewal → growth again
        
- Rest is not laziness; it improves:
    
    - **creativity**, **clarity**, and **opportunity spotting**
        
- A key problem is the expectation of **perpetual motion** (always producing, always “on”).
    

## The Two Engines Behind Hustle Culture

- **Scarcity mindset:** “If I slow down, I’ll miss my chance.”
    
    - Fueled by beliefs like: “Your twenties are your only window,” “time is running out,” “opportunities are rare.”
        
- **Urgency culture:** “Everything must happen now.”
    
    - Leads to rushed decisions, shallow work, chronic stress, and strained relationships.
        
- Golden nugget: Move at the **“speed of trust”**—fast enough to meet real deadlines, slow enough to do work well and sustainably.
    

## “Purposeful Work” Filter (A Practical Checklist)

Purposeful work should:

- Use your **zone of genius** (what you’re naturally strong at)
    
- Align with your **values**
    
- Support the **impact** you want to have
    
- Help you feel **contentment**
    
- Meet your **real needs** (time, health, boundaries, relationships)
    

If an opportunity doesn’t meet your needs, it may be a **shiny object**—close to the dream, but not the dream.

## Redefining Ambition Itself

- Ambition = **a desire for more life** (growth, unfolding, becoming).
    
- Ambition is **neutral and natural**—not inherently good or bad.
    
- The danger is not ambition; it’s ambition **“no matter the cost.”**
    

## Two Types of Ambition

### **Painful Ambition** (the harmful version)

Signs:

- **Unsustainable pace** (80-hour weeks, constant stress)
    
- “Win **no matter the cost**”
    
- Using your mind/body like tools to extract output (sleep loss, burnout as a badge)
    
- **Black-and-white thinking** (“If I’m not the best, I’m nothing”)
    
- Constantly **moving the goalpost** (never enjoying wins)
    

### **Purposeful Ambition** (the healthy version)

Signs:

- **Growth mindset** and flexible thinking
    
- Respect for your body and limits (ambition without self-harm)
    
- Working at the **right pace**, not the frantic pace
    
- More **collaboration** than ego-driven competition
    
- A focus on **contentment** while still growing
    

## Why Competition Can Poison Ambition

- Competition often comes from scarcity: “There’s only room for one of us.”
    
- This can create:
    
    - comparison spirals (especially via social media)
        
    - infighting among people who already face exclusion
        
- Golden nugget: **“When you’re captivated by purpose, you can’t be consumed by competition.”**
    

## The Hidden Driver: **Core Wounds**

Painful ambition is often fueled by unresolved emotional wounds.

- Five “core wounds” named:
    
    - **rejection**, **abandonment**, **humiliation**, **betrayal**, **injustice**
        
- These wounds create “masks” (protective behaviors). Example given:
    
    - **betrayal → control**
        
    - **rejection → avoidance**
        
- When ambition is driven by wounds, it becomes about **proving worth** instead of expressing truth.
    

## The Key Shift: From “Happiness” to **Contentment**

- Chasing “happiness” can be a trap because happiness is temporary (an emotion).
    
- Healthier goal: **contentment** = **“the knowledge of enough”** and **unconditional wholeness**.
    
- You can want growth _and_ still feel internally enough while growing.
    

## How to Slow Down Without Feeling Like You’re Falling Behind

Two main levers:

### 1) Work in your **Zone of Genius**

- People operate in:
    
    - **average zone**, **excellent zone**, **genius/exceptional zone**
        
- Genius work is more **potent** and requires less force.
    
- Small starting point: spend even **10 minutes a day** in your genius zone and increase over time.
    

### 2) Train your nervous system to feel **safe doing less**

- After long periods of hustle, slowing down can feel threatening.
    
- You may need to practice letting your body learn:
    
    - “This slower pace is safe.”
        
- Reminder that supports safety: “I don’t need to do everything. I need to do what I do best.”
    

## Permission Slips the Episode Gives You

- You don’t need to monetize everything.
    
- Purpose doesn’t have to be your job:
    
    - A **“good enough job”** can be valid if your life is rich outside work.
        
- Your worth is not dependent on output:
    
    - You are **born worthy**, and achievement doesn’t create your value.
        

## Social Impact Angle

- “Hurt people hurt people” → wounds can spread through relationships and workplaces.
    
- Doing your inner work reduces how much you pass pain onward.
    
- Healthier ambition can create a **ripple effect** in communities and culture.
    

## Final Advice for Your Twenties

- **Have as much fun as possible.**
    
    - Don’t sacrifice your twenties entirely to productivity.
        
    - Invest in experiences, friendships, joy, and play—because you’ll miss it later if you don’t.
