Play to your strength - supreme focus
Internalise the details - the Life Force
Widen your vision - the Global Perspective
Submit to the other - the Inside Out Perspective
Synthesize all forms of knowledge - the universal Man-Woman

title: Self-obsessiveness
content:
When it is time to ask for a favor or help, you must think first of appealing to people's self-interest in some way. Look at the world through their eyes, getting a sense of their needs. You must give them something valuable in exchange for helping you. 

In interaction with people, find a way to make the conversation revolve around them and their interests, all of which will go far to winning them to your side.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Self-obsessiveness.md

title: See yourself as others see you
content:
We are quick to discern the mistakes and defects of others, but when ti comes to ourselves we are too emotional to look squarely at our own. 

Begin this process by looking at negative events in our past. In dissecting these occurrences, focus on what WE did that either triggered or worsened the dynamic. In looking at several such incidents, we might begin to see a pattern that indicates a flaw in our character. 

You shall build detachment from your actions, own perceptions, own thoughts of yourself, and try place in other's shoes and see you as they do.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/See yourself as others see you.md

title: Rigidity
content:
People do not advertise their rigidity. You will only trip up against it if you try to introduce a new idea or procedure. Some in the group - the hyper-rigid - will become irritable, even panicky at the thought of any kind of change. If you press your case with logic and reason, you will tend to make them even more defensive and resistant. 

Do not fight rigidity: waste of time and you become rigid in the process. Just accept it, outwardly displaying deference to their need for order. On your own, however, you must work to maintain your open spirit, letting go of bad habits and deliberately cultivating new ideas.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Rigidity.md

title: Transfigure their ideas
content:
To learn from mentors we must be open and completely receptive to their ideas. We must fall under their spell. But if we take this too far, we become so marked by their influence that we have no internal space to incubate and develop our own voice, and we spend our lives tied to ideas that are not our own.

Even as we listen to our mentors, we need to cultivate slowly some distance from them. Begin by gently adapting their ideas to our circumstances, altering them to fit our style and inclinations. As you progress, focus on weaknesses in some of their ideas. We slowly mold their knowledge into our own shape.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Transfigure their ideas.md

title: Return to your origins
content:
Sometimes this inclination becomes clear through a particular activity that brings with it a feeling of heightened power. 

Sometimes one's true inclinations can be revealed through an encounter with an actual master. 

in order to master a field you must love the subject and feel a profound connection to it. Your interest must transcend the field itself and border on the religious. 

These childhood attractions are hard to put into words and are more like sensations - that deep wonder, sensual pleasure, power, and heightened awareness. **The importance of recognising these preverbal inclinations is that they are clear indications of an attraction that is not infected by the desires of other people.** 

Look for its traces in visceral reactions to something simple; a desire to repeat an activity that you never tired of; a subject that stimulated an unusual degree of curiosity; feeling of power attached to particular actions.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Return to your origins.md

title: Passive aggression
content:
Root cause of all passive aggression is the human fear of direct confrontation - the emotions that a conflict can churn up and the loss of control that ensues. 

Take a look at the people around suspects of passive aggression. Do these ppl act cautiously when in their presence?

Discard the exterior and focus only on their actions.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Passive aggression.md

title: Experimentation (the active mode)
content:
More active mode of experimentation. Take on more responsibilities, initiate projects, do work that exposes you to the criticisms of peers or public. The point of this is to gauge your progress and whether there are still gaps in your knowledge. Observe yourself in action and see how you respond to the judgment of others. Can you take criticism and use it constructively?

Often you must force yourself to initiate such actions or experiments *before you think you are ready*. You are testing your character, moving past your fears, and developing a sense of detachment to your work - looking at it through the eyes of others. You are getting a taste for the next phase in which what you produce will be under constant scrutiny. 

You know the apprenticeship is finished when you feel you have nothing left to learn in this environment.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Experimentation (the active mode).md

title: Inflexibility
content:
Being creative involves paradoxes. You must know your field inside and out and yet be able to question its most entrenched assumptions. 

You must be somewhat naive to entertain certain questions, and optimistic that you will solve the problem at hand. At same time, regularly doubt that you have achieved your goal and subject your work to intensive self-criticism. All of this requires flexibility, which mean you must not get too hung up on any single frame of mind.

Bend to the moment and adopt the attitude appropriate to the moment. 

In any event, you must avoid emotional extremes, and find a way to feel optimism and doubt at the same time.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Inflexibility.md

title: Laziness
content: Keep your ideas to yourself, or conceal enough of the details so that it is not possible for lazy people to steal them.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Laziness.md

title: Craft the appropriate persona
content:
People will tend to judge you based on your outward appearance. If you are not careful and simply assume that it is best to be yourself, they will begin to ascribe to you all kinds of qualities that have little to do with who you are but correspond to what they want to see. All of this can confuse you, make you feel insecure, and consume your attention. 

Internalising their judgment, you will find it hard to focus on your work. Your only protection is to turn this dynamic around by consciously molding these appearances, creating the image that suits you, and controlling people's judgments. 

At times you will find it appropriate to stand back and create some mystery around you, heightening your presence. At other times you will want to be more direct and impose a more specific appearance. 

Never settle on one image or give people the power to completely figure you out.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Craft the appropriate persona.md

title: The fact of great yield
content:
Opportunists instead of beginning with some broad goal, they go in search of some fact of great yield - a bit of empirical evidence that is strange and does not fit the paradigm, and yet is intriguing. This bit of evidence sticks out and grabs their attention, like the elongated rock. They are not sure of their goal and they do not yet have in mind an application for the fact they have uncovered, but they are open to where it will lead them. Once they dig deeply, they discover something that challenges prevailing conventions and offers endless opportunities for knowledge and application.

Although you begin with a field you understand deeply, do not allow your ming to become tethered(tied) to this discipline. 
Instead, read journals and books from all different fields. You might find anomaly in unrelated discipline that may have implications for your own. 

KEEP THE MIND COMPLETELY OPEN. 

Once found, speculate on what it could mean, this speculation guiding your subsequent research but not determining your conclusions. 

Better to look into ten such facts, with only one yielding a great discovery, than to look into twenty ideas that bring success but have trivial implications.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/The fact of great yield.md

title: Fixating on what is present, ignoring what is absent
content:
Focus also on what did *not* happen, the negative cues, what should have happened but did not. 

Here again LT might help.

Focus your ttention on some need that is not being met, on what is absent. This requires more thinking. 

Look at new and available technologies in the world and imagine how it could be applied in a much different way, meeting a need that we sense exists but that is not overly apparent. If the need is too obvious, others will already be working on it. In the end, the ability to alter our perspective is a function of our imagination. We have to learn how to imagine more possibilities than we generally consider, being as loose and radical with this process as we can. 

As you free up your mind and give it the power to alter your perspective, remember that emotions we experience have inordinate influence on how we perceive the world. 
So not only alter your mental perspective, but reverse your emotional one as well.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Fixating on what is present, ignoring what is absent.md

title: Rushing to generalities and ignoring details
content:
We tend to hurry conclusion based on minimal amounts of information. To combat this pattern we must sometimes shift our focus from the macro to the micro - placing much greater emphasis on the details. 

Try approaching a problem or idea with a much more open mind. Let your study of the details guide your thinking and shape your theories. Think of everything in nature as a kind of a hologram - the smallest part reflecting something essential about the whole.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Rushing to generalities and ignoring details.md

title: Revert to a feeling of inferiority
content:
What prevents ppl from learning are certain learning disabilities: sense of smugness, superiority whenever we enocunter something alien to our ways, as well as rigid ideas about what is real or true. 
If we feel like we know something, our mind close off to other possibilities. We see reflections of the truth we have already assumed. 

The sense of inferiority gives you hunger to learn. So when you enter a new environemtn try to revert to a childlike feeling of inferiority - that others know much more than you and that you are dependent upon them to learn. Drop all your preconceptions about an environment. Have no fears, interact with people and participate in the culture as deeply as possible.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Revert to a feeling of inferiority.md

title: The apprenticeship phase - the three steps or modes
content:
- [[Deep observation (the passive mode)]]
- [[Skills acquisition (the practice mode)]]
- [[Experimentation (the active mode)]]

They are overlapping. Think of the apprenticeship in terms of these steps.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/The apprenticeship phase - the three steps or modes.md

title: Mechanical intelligence
content:
Mechanical intelligence is not a degraded form of thinking, as compared to abstract reasoning. It is in fact the source of many of our reasoning skills and creative powers. 

Whatever you are creating or designing, you must test and use it yourself. Separating out the work will make you lose touch with its functionality. Through intense labor on your part, you gain a feel for what you are creating. 
In doing this work, you see and feel the flaws in the design. Do not look at the parts separately but at how they interact, experiencing what you produce as a whole.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Mechanical intelligence.md

title: Absorb the master's power - the mentor dynamic
content:
[[Keys to mastery 3]]

[[Strategies for deepening the mentor dynamic]]

[[Reversal 3]]
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Absorb the master's power - the mentor dynamic.md
links: Strategies for deepening the mentor dynamic

title: Grandiosity
content:
Greater danger might come from success and praise than from criticism. 

Cut out the love of attention and the pride of our ever-inflating ego. 

Never fail to understand the element of luck that always goes into success.

Public attention is a nuisance.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Grandiosity.md

title: Dimensional thinking
content: You are not in a hurry. You prefer the holistic approach. Look at the object of study from as many angles as possible, giving your thoughts added dimension.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Dimensional thinking.md

title: a - Notes Mastery - Huberman Greene
content:
This is associated with a feeling, sensation on the body and brain. Pursue that feeling, that sensation. It shall be unique and lead to euforia states of mind. Everything feels clicking together. You feel in the flow. You have delight in doing it.

The sublime experience comes from ourselves and is an experience on the doorstep of something much bigger. False sublime are like drugs.

'Do not talk about giftedness, inborn talents! One can name great men of all kinds who were very little gifted. They acquired greatness, became “geniuses” (as we put it), through qualities the lack of which no one who knew what they were.

would boast of: they all possessed that seriousness of the efficient workman which first learns to construct the parts properly before it ventures to fashion a great whole; they allowed themselves time for it, because they took more pleasure in making the little, secondary things well than in the effect of a dazzling whole.' Nietzsche

[[Discover your calling - the life's task]]

[[Submit to reality - The ideal apprenticeship]]

[[Absorb the master's power - the mentor dynamic]]

[[See people as they are - social intelligence]]

[[Awaken the dimensional mind - the creative active]]

[[Fuse the intuitive with the rational - mastery]]
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/a - Notes Mastery - Huberman Greene.md
links: Discover your calling - the life's task
Submit to reality - The ideal apprenticeship
Absorb the master's power - the mentor dynamic
See people as they are - social intelligence
Awaken the dimensional mind - the creative active
Fuse the intuitive with the rational - mastery

title: Envy
content:
People who praise you too much or who become overly friendly in the first stages of knowing you. Be wary. 

Unusual levels of insecurity in a person makes a person more prone to envy. 

Careful not to trigger it: if you are very skilled in an area occasionally display some weaknesses in another area, avoiding the great danger of appearing too perfect, too talented. 

If you are dealing with insecure types, you can display great interest in their work and even turn to them for advice. Not boast of any success, if necessary ascribe it to just good luck on your part. 

Occasionally reveal your own insecurities. Autoironia aiuta. 

Never make people feel stupid in your presence. 

In general, you might spark envy by standing out too much. Mantain nonthreatening exterior and blend in well with the group, at least until you are so successful it no longer matters.
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Envy.md

title: Alter your perspective
content:
Consider thinking as an extended form of vision that allows us to see more of the world, and creativity as the ability to expand that vision beyond conventional boundaries. 

In general we do not pay full attention to our stimuli, our brain gives us hints and we fill in the missing gaps. We do not fully analyse everything we encounter. We try see differences and comparisons. 
This is a mental shorthand useful in daily life. But when we are engaged in solving a problem or realizing an idea that we are being highly rational and thorough, but we are not aware of how deeply our thoughts fall into the same narrow grooves and the same categorizing shorthand. Creative people are those who have the capacity to resist this shorthand.

**(Lateral thinking might be useful here)**

To break out typical patterns to alter perspective:
1. [[Look at the "what" instead of the "how"]]
2. [[Rushing to generalities and ignoring details]]
3. [[Confirming paradigms and ignoring anomalies]]
4. [[Fixating on what is present, ignoring what is absent]]
file_path: Self-Help/Mastery/Alter your perspective.md
links: Look at the "what" instead of the "how"
Rushing to generalities and ignoring details
Fixating on what is present, ignoring what is absent

title: The Alchemist
content:
You don't have to see the end from the beginning. Start from where you are; clarity will come as you make progress. 

  

Hard work is not a problem. It’s not a curse at all. In fact, it’s a blessing. The good things of life are reserved for those that are willing to put in the work. 

  

The process of achieving your destiny will cost you hard work and dedication. Still, more importantly, those traits develop the character and capacity you need to live out your destiny. 

  

There is only one thing that makes a dream impossible to achieve: the fear of failure.
file_path: Self-Help/The Alchemist/Notes - The Alchemist.md

title: Ch by Ch summary - L3D
content:
### **Part I – Escaping the Two Classic “Good‑Life” Traps**

#### • **Chapter 1 – Should I Stay or Should I Go?**

– _Purpose_: Opens with farmer **Yoshi’s** lifelong choice to remain in his mountain village versus exploring elsewhere, framing the tension between stability and novelty that threads through the book .  
##### Key points  
– Staying offers comfort and identity; going invites uncertainty yet possibility—the dilemma motivates the need for a third life‑dimension beyond happiness or meaning .  
– Preview of the **three‑dimensional model**: happiness (comfort), meaning (purpose) **and** psychological richness (varied, perspective‑shifting experience).  
– Example: The Clash lyric in the chapter epigraph underscores the cost of both choices (“If I go there will be trouble…”) .

#### • **Chapter 2 – The Happiness Trap**

– _Purpose_: Shows how an over‑focus on feeling good can backfire.  
##### Key points  
– **Social pressure to display happiness** (the “Penn Face” phenomenon) masks distress and can lead to crises such as Madison Holleran’s suicide .  
– The trap has two halves: 
	(1) **Pressure to be happy**—people misread ordinary sadness as personal failure ; 
	(2) **Settling for “small happiness”**—a satisficer mindset may dull growth and curiosity .  
– Our **psychological immune system** normally restores mood; chasing quick fixes (alcohol, shopping) can impede it .  
– Research on **maximizers vs. satisficers** shows that accepting “good enough” can raise life satisfaction—but too much contentment can freeze exploration .  
– Example: Aristotle’s claim that happiness is life’s “chief concern” anchors why the pursuit is so alluring .

#### • **Chapter 3 – The Meaning Trap**

– _Purpose_: Explains how the pursuit of significance can narrow our moral lens or set unattainable standards.  
##### Key points  
– Calls to “be great” (Steve Jobs’s “don’t settle” commencement speech) elevate expectations that many will feel they miss .  
– **Meaningful lives can be parochial**: authoritarian or extremist identities score high on meaning yet harm outsiders .  
– The trap’s twin dangers: feeling inadequate if one’s impact is modest, and embracing causes that exclude others .  
– Advice: grow meaning locally first, broaden perspective to avoid ideological tunnel vision .  
– Example: Dr. Donna Adams‑Pickett’s lifelong mission to improve Black maternal health illustrates meaning pursued constructively rather than narrowly .

---

### **Part II – A Third Dimension: Psychological Richness**

#### • **Chapter 4 – A Life of Exploration**

– _Purpose_: Introduces **psychological richness** as a path to a good life distinct from happiness or meaning.  
##### Key points  
– Literary foil: Hesse’s **Narcissus** (stable monk) versus **Goldmund** (wandering artist) shows two radically different good lives; Goldmund embodies richness .  
– Obituary analyses show many lives high in richness but low in happiness or meaning, proving independence among the three dimensions .  
– Exploration can be outward (travel) or inward (intellectual journeys); both widen perspective.  
– Quote: “The world is a great book…they that never stir from home read only one page” (attributed to St. Augustine) .

#### • **Chapter 5 – Ingredients of Psychological Richness**

– _Purpose_: Defines the core components that make experiences rich.  
##### Key points  
– Five recurring ingredients: **novelty, diversity, challenge, memorability, perspective‑change** .  
– Experiment: writing about both the **best and worst** weekly events heightened perspective change and richness while lowering happiness, illustrating “duplex emotions” .  
– Emotional complexity (feeling positive _and_ negative emotions) typifies psychologically rich lives.

#### • **Chapter 6 – Who Is Rich, Psychologically Rich?**

– _Purpose_: Describes the personality and demographic predictors of richness.  
##### Key points  
– **Openness to experience** is the strongest Big‑Five trait predictor; extraversion and lower neuroticism add support .  
– Personality is **malleable**: deliberate openness exercises can expand richness over time.  
– Large surveys plus New York Times obituaries confirm that some people achieve two or all three good‑life dimensions simultaneously .

---

### **Part III – Practical Enrichers**

#### • **Chapter 7 – Playfulness**

– _Purpose_: Shows how everyday whimsy seeds richness.  
##### Key points  
– Playfulness counterbalances perfectionism; acts like taking a detour or “jumping in a puddle” inject spontaneity .  
– Interventions that **count playful moments** raise life satisfaction and cut depression for months (study referenced in text).  
– Advice: be “open to be a fool” to invite unexpected learning .

#### • **Chapter 8 – The Beauty of DIY**

– _Purpose_: Argues that hands‑on creation offsets the deadening effects of hyper‑efficiency.  
##### Key points  
– Adam Smith’s pin‑factory example shows how division of labor boosts output but can sap engagement and emotion .  
– Experiments with online “assembly lines” found specialization raised productivity but **lowered psychological richness** compared with whole‑task work .  
– DIY projects (bread‑baking, hand‑grinding coffee) re‑introduce challenge and sensory variety.

#### • **Chapter 9 – Do Aesthetic Experiences Count?**

– _Purpose_: Demonstrates that vicarious exploration through art can be as enriching as travel.  
##### Key points  
– **Narrative transportation**—losing oneself in books, films, or art—creates emotional breadth and fresh perspectives .  
– Example: _The Remains of the Day_ prompted the author to question blind loyalty and historical complicity, enriching moral viewpoint .

#### • **Chapter 10 – The Point of Exploration**

– _Purpose_: Philosophically defends curiosity‑driven living.  
##### Key points  
– Nietzsche’s **Zarathustra** praises lives that court both “peak and abyss,” warning that small, steady happiness can dull greatness .  
– Optimal living resembles **explore‑then‑exploit** algorithms in nature (great‑tit bird foraging studies, referenced in notes), balancing discovery and mastery.

#### • **Chapter 11 – Turn Adversity into a Psychologically Rich Experience**

– _Purpose_: Reframes hardship as a potent, if unwanted, enricher.  
##### Key points  
– Post‑traumatic growth research shows negative events can widen viewpoints if reflected on constructively (chapter footnotes).  
– Example: **Joy Ryan**, who at ninety‑four visited every U.S. national park after years of depression, now tells richer life stories .

---

### **Part IV – Story, Limits, and Synthesis**

#### • **Chapter 12 – A Story We Tell**

– _Purpose_: Emphasizes narrative editing as a tool for maintaining richness.  
##### Key points  
– Crafting a **hero’s‑journey arc** around crises heightens memorability and coherence .  
– Psychologist Dan McAdams’s **Life‑Story Interview** shows that identifying turning points and themes deepens self‑understanding .

#### • **Chapter 13 – Two Remaining Questions**

– _Purpose_: Addresses potential objections.  
##### Key points  
– **Can life be too rich?** Excessive residential moves in childhood predict lower well‑being, suggesting moderation .  
– **Can familiarity enrich?** Deep dives into long‑term passions (e.g., Jiro Ono perfecting sushi, artists evolving styles) show novelty within the familiar .  
– Quote: Dean Koontz warns that “too much adventure is exhausting,” underscoring balance .

#### • **Chapter 14 – A Good Life Without Regrets**

– _Purpose_: Synthesizes the tri‑dimensional model and offers pragmatic guidance.  
##### Key points  
– Happiness, meaning, and richness are **additive**; individuals can calibrate their mix at life milestones .  
– Top‑ten lessons include “Don’t be afraid of negative events” and “Be spontaneous,” translating research into daily heuristics .  
– Regrets of **inaction** loom largest; ask “In ten years, would I regret staying?” to prompt exploration .  
– Epigraph: Eugene Levy celebrates crawling out of comfort zones—an emblem of life in three dimensions .

---

### **Major Themes, Methods & Takeaways (cross‑chapter)**

- **Tri‑Dimensional Model**: Positions psychological richness alongside hedonic and eudaimonic well‑being, filling vocabulary gaps in life‑quality research .
    
- **Methodologies**  
    – _Psychologically Rich Life Questionnaire (PRLQ)_ in Appendix 1 operationalizes richness.  
    – _Obituary content analysis_ and _daily diary_ studies validate richness as empirically distinct .  
    – _Big‑Five correlations_ show openness as the core personality driver of richness .
    
- **Core lesson**: Ask three revolving questions—_Am I happy? Is my life meaningful? Is my life rich?_—and adjust by seeking **novel, challenging, perspective‑altering experiences** when richness lags.

title: Life in 3 Dimensions
content:
## Overall Architecture of _Life in Three Dimensions_

| Part | Chapters | Thematic focus                                                                                |
| ---- | -------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| I    | 1 – 3    | Why relying **only** on happiness or meaning produces “good‑life traps.”                      |
| II   | 4 – 6    | Introducing the **third dimension**—a _psychologically rich life_—and the research behind it. |
| III  | 7 – 11   | Practical “enrichers”: playfulness, DIY creativity, art, exploration, and even adversity.     |
| IV   | 12 – 14  | Story, limits, and a final synthesis of the three‑dimensional model.                          |

---

### PART I  Escaping the Two Classic “Good‑Life” Traps

**Chapter 1 Should I Stay or Should I Go?**

- Opening vignette: Farmer **Yoshi** exemplifies the pull of stability versus exploration (Ch.1 §1) .
    
- Core claim: _Choice_ between staying (comfort) and going (novelty) foreshadows the book’s tri‑dimensional model.
    

**Chapter 2 The Happiness Trap**

- Happiness is humanity’s “chief concern,” yet over‑pursuit backfires; the “Penn Face” culture and Madison Holleran’s suicide illustrate hidden costs .
    
- Mechanisms of the trap
    
    - Social pressure to display positivity fuels anxiety and unhealthy coping (Ch.2 §5) .
        
    - Maximizer vs. satisficer research shows lower happiness when we demand the “best” rather than “good enough” decisions .
        
- Empirical data: large international surveys place happiness above money, love, and health .
    

**Chapter 3 The Meaning Trap**

- Pursuing significance can narrow moral horizons; authoritarian or extremist identities boost meaning while harming others (Ch.3 §6) .
    
- Two pitfalls: 
	- (1) feeling inadequate if we don’t “change the world,” and 
	- (2) devotion to causes that exclude outsiders .
    
- Quote anchor: Steve Jobs’s “Don’t settle” speech frames meaning as relentless vocation (Ch.3 epigraph) .
    

---

### PART II  A Third Path: Psychological Richness

**Chapter 4 A Life of Exploration**

- Contrasts Hesse’s **Narcissus** (stable, meaningful) and **Goldmund** (wandering, rich) to introduce the third dimension .
    
- Author’s research: obituary analyses show people can score high on richness while low on happiness & meaning .
    

**Chapter 5 Ingredients of Psychological Richness**

- Five recurring ingredients: **novelty, diversity, challenge, memorability, perspective‑change** (Ch.5 §6) .
    
- Empirical vignette: writing about one’s “worst event” increased perspective change and richness while _lowering happiness_, showing duplex emotions enrich life .
    

**Chapter 6 Who Is Rich, Psychologically Rich?**

- Big‑Five profile: **Openness** is the strongest trait predictor; extraversion & lower neuroticism add support .
    
- Survey & obituary data confirm that richness can coexist with, or substitute for, happiness and meaning .
    
- Practical takeaway: Personality is malleable; deliberate openness exercises can foster richness (Ch.6 §4) .
    

---

### PART III  Tools for Enriching Life

**Chapter 7 Playfulness**

- Playfulness ≈ openness + extraversion; McCrae & Costa’s sentence‑completion study links whimsical answers to openness (Ch.7 §1) .
    
- Randomized “count‑your‑playful‑moments” intervention raised life satisfaction and cut depression for three months .
    
- Guidance: daily spontaneous acts (detours, puddle‑jumping) counter the seriousness of happiness/meaning pursuits .
    

**Chapter 8 The Beauty of DIY**

- Over‑specialization and productivity culture drain richness; DIY restores hands‑on novelty .
    
- Adam Smith’s pin‑factory story illustrates both the power and psychic cost of division of labor (Ch.8 §1) .
    
- Recommendation: periodically “escape the efficiency trap” by baking bread, grinding coffee, or tackling handmade projects .
    

**Chapter 9 Do Aesthetic Experiences Count?**

- Second‑hand exploration through art multiplies lived worlds: Proust, Woolf, and Roger Ebert exemplify vicarious richness .
    
- Case study: Moroccan bookseller Mohammed Aziz claims to have “lived 4,000 lives” via novels (Ch.9 §1) .
    

**Chapter 10 The Point of Exploration**

- Great‑tit foraging experiment: birds adjust explore‑then‑exploit strategies optimally—model for human curiosity .
    
- Philosophical defense: Eleanor Roosevelt’s call to “taste experience to the utmost” frames exploration as life’s purpose (epigraph) .
    

**Chapter 11 Turn Adversity into Richness**

- Nietzschean lens: hardships (“what doesn’t kill you…”) can catalyze new perspectives (Ch.11 §1) .
    
- Research on post‑traumatic growth supports adversity as an unchosen but potent enricher (footnotes in Ch.11) .
    

---

### PART IV  Story, Limits, Synthesis

**Chapter 12 A Story We Tell**

- Crafting one’s life narrative (hero’s‑journey structure) integrates chaotic episodes into coherent richness .
    

**Chapter 13 Two Remaining Questions**

- _Can there be too much richness?_ Excessive moves correlate with lower well‑being; moderation advised (Ch.13 §1) .
    
- _Can familiarity enrich?_ Novelty can be found in deep dives into established passions—e.g., repeatedly exploring the same artist’s evolving work .
    

**Chapter 14 A Good Life Without Regrets**

- Final argument: Happiness, meaning, and richness are **additive, not exclusive**; knowing values lets individuals balance the triad (Ch.14 §1 & §4) .
    
- Eugene Levy’s quote on “crawling out of comfort zones” epitomizes three‑dimensional living (Ch.14 epigraph) .
    

---

### Key Frameworks & Methodologies

|Framework / Tool|Purpose|Evidence base|
|---|---|---|
|**Tri‑Dimensional Model**(Happiness–Meaning–Psychological Richness)|Expands classic dual models of well‑being|Synthesized across Parts I–IV|
|**Psychologically Rich Life Questionnaire (PRLQ)**|12‑item scale in Appendix 1 to measure richness tendency|Validated in cross‑national samples (Appendix note in contents)|
|**Obituary Content Analysis**|Rated NY Times obits on the triad dimensions; showed independence of the three paths|Results in Ch.4 & Ch.6|
|**Big‑Five Personality Correlates**|Openness strongest predictor of richness; happiness ties to extraversion & stability; meaning to conscientiousness|Summary table in Ch.6 §2 and §“The Big Five in Five Minutes”|
|**Explore‑then‑Exploit Algorithms**|Great‑tit bird study and computational models illuminate optimal exploration|Ch.10 §1 experiment|
|**Playfulness Intervention**|Counting playful acts boosts well‑being for up to 3 months|Ch.7 empirical study|

---

### Concluding Synthesis

Shigehiro Oishi argues that a **full, regret‑lite life** is three‑dimensional. When **happiness** (comfort & positive affect) and **meaning** (purpose & contribution) plateau or even conflict, **psychological richness**—defined by _varied, perspective‑shifting experiences_—fills the void, adds narrative depth, and inoculates against monotony .

The book’s empirical spine (surveys, personality tests, field experiments, and eclectic case studies) shows that richness can be deliberately cultivated through playful openness, DIY creativity, vicarious art, mindful exploration, and meaning‑making from adversity. Crucially, the dimensions are _not zero‑sum_; many exemplars (e.g., Oliver Sacks, Linda the taxi‑driver) score high on two or all three axes .

**Take‑home principle:** Regularly ask not only _“Am I happy?”_ or _“Is my life meaningful?”_ but also _“Is my life rich?”_—and, when the answer is “no,” court novelty, diversity, and challenge until your inner story regains its multidimensional depth.

title: Specialization and Its Discontents
content:
In other words, when you are assigned to be a specialist, you lose interest in other seemingly irrelevant matters. 
When you are assigned to be a generalist, you are more curious about other things.
But why? Well, when you specialize in something, you are selectively attending to what is relevant to your specialty and trying to ignore irrelevant information.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Specialization and Its Discontents.md

title: The Personality of Happiness
content:
The essential characteristics of happy people: 
- extraverted, 
- emotionally stable (lacking neuroticism), 
- conscientious, and 
- agreeable.

A happy life is characterized by emotional stability, extraversion, conscientiousness, and agreeableness, but ***not*** by openness to experiences.

Why are emotional stability, extraversion, conscientiousness, and agreeableness good for a happy life? Primarily because the quality of social relationships is a big predictor of happiness.

Why is openness to experience uncorrelated to a happy life? One potential reason is the greater motivation to explore among open people. When an exploration (e.g., trying out a new restaurant) results in a positive outcome, it is associated with higher levels of happiness. But when the exploration results in a negative experience, it is not. Exploration is also antithetical to the stability that is the foundation for a happy life.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/The Personality of Happiness.md

title: But Negative Emotions Are Normal
content:
The happiness trap has two faces. First, there is the pressure to be happy, which makes feeling sadness, anger, and anguish seem undesirable and alienating. And yet it is not possible to avoid negative emotions. When you miss a train, when someone criticizes you, when your child shows no sign of gratitude for what you do, you feel bad. It is only natural.

Just as the biological immune system kicks in when a virus enters the body, the psychological immune system automatically kicks in when an undesirable event happens. 

What is surprising is how much people underestimate the power of their psychological immune system and instead rely heavily on their homemade remedies for mood repair, from alcohol to sweets to shopping. If we didn’t feel as much pressure to feel happy and simply let our negative moods linger a bit, our own psychological immune system would kick in, attack the “psychological virus” of unhappiness, and remove it. This element of the happiness trap leads us down a potentially destructive path of unhealthy coping.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/But Negative Emotions Are Normal.md

title: Finding Richness in the Familiar?
content: “In music an even tempo may have much beauty and great effect”—even if it is monotonic. That is, marital love might suffer from monotony, but monotony can be an even tempo that, played properly, produces beauty and great effect.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Finding Richness in the Familiar?.md

title: Study Abroad!
content:
Psychological richness requires a lot of ingredients, not just novelty but also challenges and perspective change.

Perhaps artistic activities challenge you to expand your perspective more than shopping, partying, or taking a short trip.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Study Abroad!.md

title: To Sum Up
content:
- Novelty: there’s something different from the same old, same old. 

- Diversity: a wide range of attention and emotion is deployed. 

- Challenge: life is more difficult and complex than usual. 

- Memorable: life is vivid. Above all, you learn something new.

Simplifying one’s life so as to have reliably positive experiences, or contentment, is key to **happiness**. 
Dedicating one’s life to others with compassion is key to **meaning**. 
Experiencing the unusual, challenging oneself, and learning new things—though frustrating and unpleasant at times—are key to **psychological richness**.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/To Sum Up.md

title: Love It or List It
content:
This is the psychology of ownership, or the endowment effect. Once you own something, the value of that object increases to you. This is why most people overestimate how valuable their houses are. Because they own it and are familiar with it, they like it and think it is more valuable than most others would—another

The lesson here is that we like the idea of variety, but in the end we tend to choose the same old products.

When faced with an actual choice, we tend to choose a sure “winner” rather than a risky product. We’ll settle for something familiar because trying something new is too much work. In the long view, we want novelty; in the short term, we want security. Familiarity is warm and fuzzy, and it has a surprising power over us, steering us away from psychological richness.

We want a sure gain rather than a hypothetical gain, even when the hypothetical gain could be larger than the sure gain. A sure win is pleasant and comfortable. A hypothetical win is risky, anxiety- provoking, and uncomfortable. Loss aversion makes us conservative in many decision-making situations. But if we always chose a sure bet, we would never venture outside of our comfort zone. If we never ventured outside of our comfort zone, we would never encounter anything unexpected.

We tend to underestimate how enjoyable doing something unfamiliar, like talking to a stranger, is.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Love It or List It.md

title: The Many Benefits of Exploration
content: Time spent living abroad also helps individuals gain a clearer sense of who they are and the lives they want to pursue. Individuals who have more foreign experiences trust others more and are better able to understand and relate to others. Those who have explored the world tend to hold fewer negative stereotypes and prejudices, and they discriminate against outgroups members less than those who have not.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/The Many Benefits of Exploration.md

title: Serious Business
content:
How can those of us who don’t have those basic personality traits become more open and extraverted? One answer is to embrace playfulness.

“The final distinguishing characteristic of open men is a playful, sometimes odd, sense of humor. A slight twist may be given to meanings, and obviously playful objections to the test are frequently made.”

When you are playful, you are “on vacation from social and economic reality.”

Early specialization might dispel interest in other activities in life and could lead to early burnout. The high prevalence of burnout and depression among elite athletes and academics makes me wonder if a singular grit needs to be paired with playfulness.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Serious Business.md

title: Set Yourself Free from the Productivity Trap!
content:
Being a generalist requires broader perspectives than being a specialist. Familiarity generates reliable happiness, but it doesn’t generate much richness. In contrast, while a challenging project can produce a lot of negative emotions in the short run, it can also add psychological richness in the long run.

Before hiring someone else to do a job, ask yourself: Could I do it myself? Would I have an interesting story to tell if I did? Is efficiency everything? Escape the productivity trap once in a while. 

Think of a slower way to do the same thing—bake your own bread; hand-grind your own coffee beans…do it yourself, and add some spice to your life.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Set Yourself Free from the Productivity Trap!.md

title: The Way You Want It
content:
If you don’t think of the world the way you want it, you forget it is your life. 
So, let us not forget that this is our life and that we want something more than what it is.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/The Way You Want It.md

title: The Big Five in Five Minutes
content:
Personality is often defined as characteristic patterns of thinking, feeling, and behavior

Goldberg’s study and others like it found that many personality traits cluster neatly into the following five factors: 
- **extraversion** (e.g., jolly, talkative, sociable, active), 
- **neuroticism** (e.g., touchy, fearful, self-critical), 
- **conscientiousness** (e.g., persistent, tidy, dependable), 
- **agreeableness** (e.g., generous, kind, trustful), 
- **openness** to experience (e.g., literary, insightful, perceptive).
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/The Big Five in Five Minutes.md

title: Life’s Chief Concern
content:
The irony is that our blind pursuit of happiness sometimes leads to unhappiness.

If happiness is a victory, the pursuit of happiness is equated with the pursuit of a victory. Success brings happiness. Failure begets unhappiness. To many, if you are not happy, you are not successful. If you are unhappy, you are a loser.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Life’s Chief Concern.md

title: A Twenty-Four-Hour Live Concert - Too Much Stimulation?
content:
In the end, though, we can’t know what is too much for us until we try.
We have an instinct to run away from risks, which helps us survive. But too much running away makes us too timid to truly live. We need to push ourselves beyond our comfort zones every once in a while.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/A Twenty-Four-Hour Live Concert - Too Much Stimulation?.md

title: Narcissus or Goldmund?
content:
It isn’t big wins that bring happiness, but rather the small joys in life, like having tea with your best friend. It is not career success but rather interpersonal success that typically leads to long-term happiness. 
Similarly, meaning in life comes from dedication to a particular cause you care about dearly and making a difference in that chosen realm. 
Overall, a life of stability, rather than a life of exploration and change, gets you closer to happiness and meaning in life.

According to Hesse, a life of exploration is a good life, even at the cost of much happiness or meaning.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Narcissus or Goldmund?.md

title: Jiro - The Art of Lifelong Devotion
content: One way to reach psychological richness is to follow Jiro’s path: devotion to one career, provided that the chosen career is deep enough for lifelong exploration.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Jiro - The Art of Lifelong Devotion.md

title: The Role of Extraversion
content:
The easiest way to think about the link is to conceive of extraversion as an interest in exploring the social world. That is, if openness to experience is concerned with the exploration of sensory and abstract information, extraversion is concerned with the exploration of interpersonal information.
If open people are curious about ideas, extraverts are curious about other people. Because people are different, the more people you interact with, the more diverse experiences you will have and the richer your life becomes.

In the long run, then, extraverts are more likely to meet different kinds of people and are more likely to learn new things than an introvert like myself. This is one way that extraversion is linked to a psychologically rich life. 

On a basic level, why do extraverts explore the social world more? One factor is their confidence and self-assurance. When you are confident, meeting a new person is not threatening. What is there to lose? You are not worried about being negatively evaluated by this person or making a bad impression. Instead, you are thinking about the benefits: getting to know someone new, learning something new, sharing interesting stories.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/The Role of Extraversion.md

title: The Personality of Meaningfulness
content:
Personality research has shown that people who lead a meaningful life are characterized first by 
- conscientiousness, followed by 
- emotional stability, 
- extraversion,
- agreeableness, and, as with happy people, least by 
- openness to experience. 

Conscientious people are well organized, careful, and thorough. They have clear goals and pursue them diligently.

So long as one’s neuroticism is not overwhelming, a meaningful life is possible.

In contrast, openness is difficult for those who have strong convictions. It’s easy to become self-righteous about your causes.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/The Personality of Meaningfulness.md

title: What Is a Good Vacation?
content:
Some friends are fun to be with. 
Other friends are useful and helpful. 
Other friends help you expand your horizons.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/What Is a Good Vacation?.md

title: Spontaneity
content:
Like playfulness, spontaneity can enrich our lives.

Here comes a hard question: When was the last time you went out with someone spontaneously?
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Spontaneity.md

title: What the Great Tit Knows
content: By definition, a psychologically rich life leans toward “exploration” strategies rather than “exploitation” strategies.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/What the Great Tit Knows.md

title: A Lesson from Personality Psychology
content:
So, who is likely to lead a psychologically rich life? In terms of personality traits, people who are open to experience and extraverted are most likely to lead a psychologically rich life. They are more likely to do something new, meet someone new, or learn something new. 

Who is happy? Extraverts and emotionally stable people tend to lead a happy life perhaps in part because they tend to have good relationships and find something positive in most situations. Conscientious people tend to be happy as well, perhaps because they tend to achieve their goals, whereas agreeable people tend to be happy in part because they have warm, conflict-free relationships with others.

Finally, who leads a meaningful life? Conscientious and emotionally stable people tend to do so in part because they can devote themselves to a few causes for an extended period of time without worrying too much, and in the end make a difference in the world.

A lesson from personality psychology, then, is that it is important to know who you are (you can take a Big Five personality test on my website!).
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/A Lesson from Personality Psychology.md

title: An Interesting Life vs. an Interesting Story
content: Psychological richness is inherently intertwined with the quantity and quality of your stories. The more interesting stories you have, the more psychologically rich your life is.

title: What About Outsourcing?
content: Ashley Whillans and colleagues found that, across 4,469 respondents, those who spent some money every month to save free time were happier than those who didn’t. This was in part explained by time stress measured by prompts such as, “I feel pressed for time today.” That is, those who spent money on time-saving services were less stressed about time, and the less stressed about time someone is, the more satisfied they are with their lives overall.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/What About Outsourcing?.md

title: This Book Is About…
content:
Psychological richness is different from happiness and meaning in the sense that it is not about an overall feeling of where life is going or what the point of your life is, but about an experience, or more precisely the accumulation of experiences over time.

The more interesting experiences and stories you have, the more psychologically rich you are. Just as you can accumulate wealth and become materially rich, you can accumulate experiences and become psychologically rich.

![[Pasted image 20250924101335.png]]

A psychologically rich life is not for everyone. It suits the curious more than the content.

The comfort and security of a happy or meaningful life provide a safety net that a psychologically rich life, with all its unknowns, often lacks. Yet the paradox of happiness and meaning is that the complacency they foster can make for an incomplete life with major regrets, doubts, and unanswered questions.

By reminding ourselves that what counts is not just the destination but also the journey, we learn to find value in seeking new experiences and new knowledge, hopefully leading to a life without regrets, or at least fewer regrets.

For those who stay, psychological richness will add a new dimension of interest and resilience to life.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/This Book Is About….md

title: What We Can Learn from Others’ Struggles
content:
Wilson and Linville’s experiment shows that having a role model—knowing someone who went through similar, tough experiences but ended up successful— can provide the idea and hope that it is possible to improve and survive.

For me, the main lesson of Redirect is that our story is in our control. You can see your struggles from a different perspective, and the new interpretation of those struggles becomes a foundational step toward self-understanding and further transformation. 

Edit your story, drop the old version of yourself, and come to believe in the new version of your narrative. I am not saying that you should edit out all the bad experiences to create a happier version of your life story. Not at all. You should keep most, if not all, of the bad experiences, but use them as a springboard for later transformation, as a point of redirection in your life story. 

You can’t make an uneventful life psychologically rich.

At the heart of a psychologically rich life is experience itself. Unless you have a life full of interesting experiences, it is not going to be a psychologically rich one. But if you become a better “story editor,” you can extract the most interesting aspects of your experiences and make your story psychologically richer.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/What We Can Learn from Others’ Struggles.md

Your work is going to fill a large part of your life, and the only way to be truly satisfied is to do what you believe is great work. And the only way to do great work is to love what you do. If you haven’t found it yet, keep looking. Don’t settle. As with all matters of the heart, you’ll know when you find it.

title: What Is a Psychologically Rich Life?
content: A psychologically rich life is a life filled with diverse, unusual, interesting experiences that change your perspective; a life with twists and turns; a dramatic, eventful life instead of a simple and straightforward one; a life with multiplicity and complexity; a life with lots of stops, detours, and turning points; a life that feels like a long, winding hike rather than many laps of the same racing circuit.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/What Is a Psychologically Rich Life?.md

title: Psychologically Rich Films
content:
What is the key to a psychologically rich aesthetic experience? 

First, while reading or watching, one gets so immersed in the narrative world described in the story that they don’t notice minor changes in the physical world around them (e.g., someone entering the room): a complete melding of attention, imagery, and feeling that psychologists call “transportation.”

Second, just as in a physical experience, not every immersive experience is psychologically rich. It also has to be memorable.

In order for the reading or watching experience to be rich, it has to remain vivid in one’s memory. In other words, unless these experiences are retained in your mind, they are not enriching your life.

Lastly, in order for reading and watching to be rich, it has to have some complexity and eventually change your perspective.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Psychologically Rich Films.md

title: How to Keep the Fire
content:
Aron’s well-known “self-expansion theory” of romantic relationships posits that, at the beginning of a relationship, two individuals engage in intense conversations with a lot of self-disclosure; as time passes, their partner becomes part of themselves, essentially expanding the self. 

However, after the initial phase of mutual self-disclosure, opportunities for further expansion are limited. Aron theorizes that the typical decline in marital satisfaction over the course of a marriage is due in part to this lack of new opportunities to expand the self, and that engagement in novel activities could provide further opportunities for self-expansion. In this way, couples can experience “continuous rejuvenation.”
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/How to Keep the Fire.md

title: How to Transcend the Happiness Trap
content:
If you are the type of person who often accepts “good enough,” also known as a “satisficer”—one who sacrifices the best choice for a satisfactory choice—you are much more likely to be happy with your decisions.

Sonja Lyubomirsky and Lee Ross found that individuals who do not engage in upward social comparison are happier than those who are obsessed with social comparison.

Since Denmark often ranks as one of the happiest countries in the world, the BBC went to the Danes in search of the key to happiness. The secret? Many Danes said they don’t expect much in life. Lower your expectations. Be content with what you have, Danish wisdom advised.

This is the second part of the happiness trap: settling for less than you can achieve.

If you feel pressure to feel happy all the time, remember that it is OK to be sad, angry, or fearful every now and then. These emotions add complexity and richness to your inner life. And if you’re already happy with your life, you’re doing great. But take a moment to reflect on your satisficer mindset. Perhaps there could be more to life than coziness and small joys, at least once in a while. Happiness is not the only way to lead a good life.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/How to Transcend the Happiness Trap.md

title: Chapter 11 - Turn Adversity Into a Psychologically Rich Experience
content:
A painting by Guido Reni shows St. Michael putting his foot on Satan’s neck. William James commented on this painting, saying that “the world is all the richer for having a devil in it, so long as we keep our foot upon his neck.” 

Psychological richness and misfortune can coexist, so long as the misfortune is kept in check.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Chapter 11 - Turn Adversity Into a Psychologically Rich Experience.md

title: Why Aesthetic Experiences Matter
content: At the end of Time Regained, the final volume of In Search of Lost Time, Proust talks about the role of art more generally: “Through art alone are we able to emerge from ourselves, to know what another person sees of a universe which is not the same as our own and of which, without art, the landscapes would remain as unknown to us as those that may exist on the moon. Thanks to art, instead of seeing one world only, our own, we see that world multiply itself and we have at our disposal as many worlds as there are original artists, worlds more different one from the other than those which revolve in infinite space, worlds which, centuries after the extinction of the fire from which their light first emanated, whether it is called Rembrandt or Vermeer, send us still each one its special radiance.”
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Why Aesthetic Experiences Matter.md

title: Bittersweet
content:
Susan Cain contends that “light and dark, birth and death—bitter and sweet—are forever paired” and that negative emotions such as sorrow and longing “make us whole.” She argues that negative emotions play a central role in our emotional lives and make us more empathetic and even more creative. Similarly, I think that negative emotions such as sorrow and longing, along with positive emotions, make our lives psychologically richer.

Those who were leading a happy life experienced a lot of joy, contentment, and pleasantness but infrequently experienced sadness, anger, and fear. 
Those who were leading a meaningful life were quite similar to those leading a happy life in that they had more positive emotions than negative emotions. 
Interestingly, those who were leading a psychologically rich life experienced a lot of positive emotions but also experienced negative emotions quite a bit.

Those who wrote about both the best and the worst events reported a greater degree of perspective change. Writing about the worst event, in short, increased the likelihood of perspective change. Most important, the more perspective changes they reported, the psychologically richer they reported their lives to be. Interestingly, the more perspective change they reported, the less happiness they reported. Just as negative emotions such as fear and jealousy add drama to books and movies, the worst event had induced perspective change and made the participants’ lives psychologically richer.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Bittersweet.md

title: The Utility of a Third Dimension
content:
Happiness and meaning are both important. But they do not capture an adventurous, unconventional, and dramatic life

How, then, is psychological richness different from happiness and meaning?
Happiness is a subjective feeling that rises and falls to indicate where one’s life stands. It is a bit like a balloon. With the right wind and air pressure, it floats high. Smooth sailing. Life is going well. But when the weather is bad, it deflates. Grounded and stuck. Life is not going well.

Meaning in life, on the other hand, boils down to whether your life has a “point. ” When you’re devoted to making a difference in the world, your life certainly has a point. You see the fruits of your labor, your legacy. There is a reason for your existence. But when your efforts are not making a clear difference, it is harder to see the point of your life.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/The Utility of a Third Dimension.md

title: The Pressure to Be Happy
content: Too much emphasis on happiness has led to a dangerous misconception that unhappiness is a sign of failure. In one experiment, participants were asked to solve anagrams with or without the pressure to be happy. The anagrams were impossible to solve, and everyone failed. The participants who were pressured to be happy ruminated about the failure more than the participants in the control condition, where there was no pressure to be happy. The same failure feels worse when you believe that you are supposed to be happy.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/The Pressure to Be Happy.md

title: Happiness Is a Bore?
content:
One common critique is that a happy life might be a selfish one. In reality, there is lots of evidence that suggests otherwise. For example, spending money on others as opposed to on oneself increases happiness. Happy people volunteer more than unhappy people. 

But for now let’s assume the critique of happiness-as-selfishness and proceed with a question: What makes for a good life, if not happiness? 
The novelist Donna Tartt suggested that it is “to make other people happy even at the expense of one’s own happiness”—in other words, what many scholars call a “meaningful life.”

Meaning in life is typically defined by significance, purpose, and coherence. 
1. First, a meaningful life is a life that matters. It matters not just to one’s family members and friends, but also to strangers. A meaningful life is a life that makes a difference in the world.
2. Second, a meaningful life has a clear purpose. A person who leads a meaningful life knows where they’re going. There is a clear sense of direction and a guiding principle.
3. Third, a meaningful life is well organized.

In contrast, a meaningless life is a life that does not make any difference in the world.

There are millions of people around the world who are toiling away their lives in meaningless jobs. He defines a bullshit job as a job that the person who is doing it can’t really justify the existence of that job.

Of course, people can make a difference in the world outside of their jobs. So this doesn’t mean corporate lawyers and public relations consultants lead meaningless lives. But if you are not making any positive difference in the world, your life may feel pointless.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Happiness Is a Bore?.md

title: A Psychologically Rich Day via Poetry
content:
In his poem “Happiness,” Raymond Carver describes his morning routines. He is sitting by the window with a cup of coffee and looking out. He sees the newspaper boy with his friend walking up to his driveway, just smiling: “Happiness. It comes on unexpectedly. And goes beyond, really, any early morning talk about it.” Carver finds happiness in this mundane scene. This is a predictable, repeatable scene. Yet it is also sudden and ephemeral. If you don’t pay attention, you might miss it. A happy day comes from noticing something heartwarming, having a good laugh with friends, and, according to Carver, not thinking about death, ambition, love, or anything too deep.

A meaningless day is a day wasted, an utterly pointless day. Like listening to a lecture on material you already know well, a meaningless day does not add much to your life. A meaningful day is the opposite. There is a point to that day. You’ve accomplished something. Maybe you wrote a letter, for instance. You helped someone. You did laundry or exercised. You got something done, and the day was not totally wasted. Even if you didn’t get much done, maybe you got some much-needed rest to prepare you for another day of hard work. There are many ways to make a day meaningful.

A meaningful day is not just a day with explicit gains. 
***It is also a day you appreciate because “one day, I know, it will be otherwise.”***

A psychologically rich day is a day when you experience something unfamiliar, feel a wide range of emotions, and gain a new perspective on life.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/A Psychologically Rich Day via Poetry.md

title: The Personality of Psychological Richness
content:
Two main personality predictors of a psychologically rich life were 
- **openness to experiences** and 
- **extraversion**.

Open people are imaginative, curious, and interested in intellectual and artistic pursuits. 
People low in openness are conventional and down-to-earth, preferring routine and less interested in intellectual or artistic pursuits.

There is a reciprocal relationship between openness to experience and psychological richness, such that openness to experience increases one’s chance of engaging in richness-enhancing activities, which in turn boosts one’s openness further.

Personality psychologists have classified openness to experience into two subfactors: 
**openness** and **intellect**. 
People high in openness tend to have more creative accomplishments in the arts, whereas people high in intellect tend to have more creative accomplishments both in arts and sciences.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/The Personality of Psychological Richness.md

title: Be Great?
content:
Like the happiness trap, there is also a meaning trap. 
The first element of the meaning trap is that the type of accomplishment associated with a meaningful life is so grand that aiming for it will set us up for a failure.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Be Great?.md

title: Why You Learn Something New from the Old
content: We often learn something new from studying something very old.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Why You Learn Something New from the Old.md

title: How to Avoid the Meaning Trap
content:
Like the happiness trap, the meaning trap has two parts. 

1. First, for the majority of us who have not invented anything or participated in the Peace Corps, it is easy to feel like a failure in the face of the call to be great. Pressure to make a difference in the world can be emotionally taxing, just like the pressure to be happy. Most of us feel that way once in a while. If you feel like you haven’t made any meaningful difference in the world, consider that you could achieve meaning through devotion to a certain cause, starting out small in your neighborhood.

2. The second part of the meaning trap is that the pursuit of a meaningful life can promote a narrow viewpoint. As research shows, meaning in life is sometimes attained through prosocial behaviors toward a small ingroup, and antisocial behaviors or apathy toward outgroups. If you lead a meaningful life in a narrow sense, you might want to consider expanding your perspective.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/How to Avoid the Meaning Trap.md

title: Too Many Moves?
content: The key takeaway from our paper was that excessive childhood moves could have a lingering negative effect on subjective well-being in adulthood among introverts because they tend to have a harder time making social connections in new places and end up with few close relationships in adulthood.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Too Many Moves?.md

title: So What Does Increase Happiness?
content:
A victory rarely results in everlasting (or even long- lasting) happiness.

We tend to overestimate the happiness that our success will bring about, while also overestimating the unhappiness that our failure will generate.

People overestimate the role of success in their own happiness.
It is not a big win that brings happiness. Rather, it’s the small things in life, like having a coffee with your best friend every day, that build long-term happiness. Happiness is the frequency, not the intensity, of positive events.
Studies show that happiness is the product of close relationships rather than personal accomplishments.
That is, happiness is not personal success, but interpersonal success.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/So What Does Increase Happiness?.md

title: Life Is Pretty Meaningful. But Why?
content:
One way to find meaning is by following a traditional religion. 

Steger’s research also finds that people who say they have a meaningful life tend to be optimistic about the future, extraverted, non-neurotic, agreeable, conscientious, and have high self-esteem. Meaning in life is by nature very subjective. Some well-respected, well-liked, award-winning scientists nonetheless see their lives to be meaningless and die by suicide.

Second, people with high self-esteem are more likely to say that their lives matter than those who do not like themselves. Non-neurotic people (those who don’t worry too much or are not too stress prone) are more likely to say that their lives have a clear purpose and direction than neurotic people. Conscientious people achieve more of their goals than unconscientious people. Therefore, they are also more likely to feel that they are moving toward a larger goal in life than unconscientious people. They are likely to feel that their lives have a sense of direction, purpose, and meaning.

People tend to choose one or two causes they care about dearly (e.g., a soup kitchen, a church), volunteer in the same location for an extended period of time, and achieve meaning by trying to make a difference in a well-defined area. Others derive meaning in life from their work, family, and community, again a narrowly defined realm of life. Research on meaning in life shows that successfully achieving a meaningful life often comes with focus and narrowness. In most cases, this is not a problem.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Life Is Pretty Meaningful. But Why?.md

title: Quantifying the Psychologically Rich Life
content:
A happy life: a life in which a person is comfortable, satisfied, and joyful

A meaningful life: a life in which a person makes a contribution to society and makes a difference

A psychologically rich life: a life in which a person encounters many diverse, unusual experiences
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Quantifying the Psychologically Rich Life.md

title: A Psychologically Rich Day - Data
content:
It turned out that doing something new, eating something new, or meeting someone new enhanced not only psychological richness but also happiness and meaning. That is, most students reported that their day was happier, more meaningful, and richer on a day when they did something new, ate something new, or met someone new.

Second, we found some unique predictors of a psychologically rich day. For instance, an atypical day was psychologically richer than a typical day, but an atypical day was not happier than a typical day. A day with more free time was psychologically richer than a day with less free time. In contrast, a free day was no more meaningful than a less free day. Finally, a day with more required work was happier and more meaningful than a day with less required work.

So getting things done made the day happier and more meaningful, whereas doing something unusual made the day psychologically richer.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/A Psychologically Rich Day - Data.md

title: Why Do We Need Playfulness?
content:
Personality traits alone do not determine our capacity for psychological richness: we can all learn to foster psychological richness through playfulness and spontaneity.

As an antidote to too much seriousness, we need playfulness and spontaneity. 
Hobby over duty. 
Entertainment over commitment. 
Relaxation over dedication. 
Try to be playful at work or at home at least once a day. Try to make your partner, friend, or coworker laugh. Take a detour on your way home. Jump in a puddle. Go for a swim. Get wet; get dirty. Be like a little kid once in a while. When you do something spontaneous, you’ll make life rich and memorable.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Why Do We Need Playfulness?.md

title: The Adventure of Alison in Wonderland
content: “Once again, I was an exceptionally fortunate and happy woman, full of irrational exuberance and everyday joy. But that’s not all I was. I’d discovered that I could love women as well as men, history as well as science, and that I could make my way through sadness and solitude, not just happiness…. I had found my salvation in the sheer endless curiosity of the human mind—and the sheer endless variety of human experience.”
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/The Adventure of Alison in Wonderland.md

title: Build Your Portfolio of Psychological Richness
content:
Memory is key to a psychologically rich life. If we forget a past event, we miss out on the chance to add that experience to our portfolio of psychological wealth. But how do we remember? Looking to cognitive science, it’s clear that deep processing, rehearsal, and consolidation are the keys. In other words, we need to pay attention to what is happening, think about an event frequently, and elaborate on it in order to remember it.

Without reflection, experience serves as a momentary thrill.

A good listener not only asks the right questions and listens, but also helps you craft a story and see the point of your story.

By emphasizing struggles and challenges, you can give your narrative a structure and transform it. You can make the same story more like a hero’s journey by focusing on crises and how you overcame them.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Build Your Portfolio of Psychological Richness.md

title: Key to Narrative Complexity
content: The way you view your role in your own life—whether you see yourself as a hero of the story or as an observer—does make a difference in terms of how the story is told and whether your life sounds more satisfying, more meaningful, and, most likely, psychologically richer.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Key to Narrative Complexity.md

title: Lessons of Psychological Richness
content:
It’s easy to come up with an excuse for inaction, but when faced with a question of “Should I stay or should I go?” ask yourself: “In ten years, would I regret if I stayed?” 

Remember, we may regret what we do in the short run, but in the long run, our biggest regrets come from what we didn’t do.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Lessons of Psychological Richness.md

title: Grace and Rachel’s Rich Weekend
content: Psychologically rich experiences involve not only novelty but also intensity, complexity, and a change in perspective.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Grace and Rachel’s Rich Weekend.md

title: The Mere Exposure Effect Goes Shopping
content:
So, a familiar person or store seems to be particularly attractive when you are away from familiar places and surrounded by strangers.

Stress and anxiety amplify our tendency to like a familiar object.

Psychological research shows that our tendency to like a familiar object and our aversion to extra effort makes us settle down prematurely. Our own research further shows that this bad mental habit gets worse when we are under stress.

Familiarity is a powerful force. It gives us comfort. It’s a psychological teddy bear. The mere exposure effect exerts its strongest effect, and a psychological teddy bear is most attractive, paradoxically and unfortunately, when we are exploring the world and putting ourselves in uncomfortable situations.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/The Mere Exposure Effect Goes Shopping.md

title: Be Playful
content: René Proyer has studied adult playfulness more intensely than anyone I know. He found that playfulness is a blend of openness to experience and extraversion, and a relative lack of conscientiousness and neuroticism. It is “an openness to being a fool, which is a combination of not worrying about competence, not being self-important, not taking norms as sacred and finding ambiguity and double edges a source of wisdom and delight.”
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Be Playful.md

title: Happiness, Meaning, and Something Else
content:
psychological research shows that trying to make others happy will make you happy, while trying to make yourself happy sometimes fails to do so.

In the short term, people regret action, like saying or doing something stupid. In the long run, however, people regret inaction, like not saying “I love you” or not going back to school. Some people may lead a life of self-sacrifice and virtue but forgo opportunities that ultimately lead to more regrets and “what ifs.”

Self-sacrifice is admirable, to be sure, but prioritizing it can lead people to lose sight of their own desires and ideals until their lives no longer feel authentic.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Happiness, Meaning, and Something Else.md

title: Finally Some Data
content: Lionel Trilling’s main thesis in his 1950 book The Liberal Imagination: “Literature is the human activity that takes the fullest and most precise account of variousness, possibilities, complexity, and difficulty.” Our studies were correlational, so the causal role of reading literature needs to be tested in the future. Overall, reading literature is associated with more cognitive complexity and better perspective-taking skills (skills and abilities to see another person’s behavior from that person’s perspective rather than from an observer’s perspective), and it appears to help us appreciate the ups and downs of our own lives.
file_path: Self-Help/Life in 3 Dimensions/files/Finally Some Data.md

title: Chapter 4 - A Life of Exploration

title: Why the Beatles?
content:
Listening to the same band for the sake of nostalgia does not increase psychological richness. 

In contrast, if you keep listening to the same band because you keep noticing something you didn’t notice before, that is a psychologically rich experience, one of finding richness in a familiar object.

title: So End the Chatter
content:
Kant defines an aesthetic experience as something greater than the enjoyment of mere sensation but rather the enjoyment of what he calls “reflective judgment,” which involves some gap between sensation and reality and a new mental representation and understanding of an object.

When Sonny Rollins, the legendary jazz saxophonist, was asked about art and, more specifically, why art matters, his reply evoked Kantian aesthetic judgments: “Technology is no savior. We can eat, sleep, look at screens, make money—all aspects of our physical existence—but that doesn’t mean anything. Art is the exact opposite. It’s infinite, and without it, the world wouldn’t exist as it does. It represents the immaterial soul: intuition, that which we feel in our hearts. Art matters today more than ever because it outlives the contentious political veneer that is cast over everything…. Art, in the same way, both inspires us to go out and find something new and highlights what we don’t know.”

Brooks writes, “Like a lot of people, I spend too much of my time enmeshed in politics…. So I’m trying to take countermeasures. I flee to the arts.” Art, he says, “prompts you to stop in your tracks, take a breath and open yourself up so that you can receive what it is offering, often with a kind of childlike awe and reverence…artworks widen your emotional repertoire. When you read a poem or see a piece of sculpture, you haven’t learned a new fact, but you’ve had a new experience.”

Rothko once said, “A painting is not about an experience. It is an experience.”
